name: Laiquendi (Green-elves)
description: Green-elves of Ossiriand.

name: Ground Bees
description: Striped with bright yellow and black like their distant cousins the yellowjackets, but fuzzy like any true bee, Ground Bees make their nests in abandoned burrows previously belonging to such creatures as Rabbits, Ground Squirrels, and Culcarnix. They are social insects who present a hazard because of their numbers and astounding coordination. Worse, their carefully hidden nests are hard (-20) to spot; when any unfortunate being steps in or near the nest, the Ground Bees, warned by the vibrations, swarm out and mob the unlucky intruder. Multiple stings can cause temporary weakness and even paralysis. Like other Bees, Ground Bees produce honey, but their underground location makes the prospect of stunning the Bees with smoke and harvesting the honey rather unattractive, so any Ground Bees that have made their nests far from Bear-infested regions (such as forests or hills) can hoard their treasure unmolested.

name: Culcarnix
description: This large, ground-dwelling rodent is found on the Talath Harroch, the plain south of Mirkwood. They are reported to be totally harmless, an opinion which owes much to the lack of farmsteads in the Talath Harroch. Culcarnix can devastate a farm field. Nevertheless, they are totally unaggressive toward other living beings. Tailless and blunt-nosed, Culcarnix live in burrows beneath the earth, and can occasionally be seen standing on the mounds of earth by the entrances to their burrows, sniffing the air for any scent of approaching enemies. They have thick, red-brown fur that has at various times been much in demand by furriers. The Wainriders, for instance, raised them in great numbers for their hides. Thus, they have become scarce in areas where there is widespread settlement.

name: Giants
description: Massive humanoid creatures found in mountains and wilderness.

name: Fen Boars
description: Smaller than their forest-dwelling relatives that gorge on autumn nuts, Fen Boars make for good hunting and tasty eating. Males can be dangerous if cornered, lashing with the long tusks they normally use to dig mud for roots and grub out rotten meat. More than other pigs they relish berries, insects and fish alongside their usual vegetarian diet.

name: Grey Wolves
description: Powerful, muscular dog (S. Draugwath, Q. Mistanarmo) with a thick coat and bushy tail, ranging all over Middle-earth and varying from near-black in warm locales to almost white in the North. Exceptionally social, living in family groups or packs that hunt cooperatively, favouring Deer and Sheep in warm climes and Caru or Losrandir in cooler ones, with rodents, fish and crustaceans besides. Mated pairs stay together for life and rarely fight over mates.

name: Horses of Rohan
description: Horses bred and ridden by the Rohirrim, known for their speed and endurance.

name: Wargs
description: Wargs are bred from cursed Wolves. Unusually large and fierce, they are also more intelligent than their canine ancestors. They serve as mounts for Orcs and as guards and scouts for Morgoth. Being artificially long-lived, undead, the spirit that inhabits a Warg's form dissipates when the body is slain. In turn, the body disappears.

name: Fell Beasts
description: Small, Dragon-related monsters, Fell Beasts are horrible and terrifying creatures bred from Winged-drakes. Sauron found them roosting in the remote highlands of the North and refined them in order to produce swift, aerial steeds for his Nazgûl.

name: Merchaithrais
description: The Shadowhorn, or "Morchaithras" (pl. "Merchaithrais" or "Morchaithrais"), is a large, flat, black Ray equipped with a long, whiplike tail. Its tail is studded with short but exceptionally sharp barbs that deliver knifelike cuts and inject victims with a powerful (3rd lvl) muscle venom. The Ray expanded pectoral fins form triangular wings that extend along its head and trunk, giving the fish a diamond shape. The tips of these wings are equipped with electricity-producing organs that can stun or immobilize a swimmer. Merchaithrais prefer to swim along the bottom of the coastal shelf, burrowing themselves in the sand near shoals in order to await their prey. While swimming, they flap their pectoral fins and move gracefully and quickly through the water, as if flying.

name: Marsh Ponies
description: Also called the Perroch-i-Lin, the Marsh Pony is a short, sturdy beast with relatively wide hooves and powerful lungs. Most are grey, and all have a long, silky mane.

name: Mearas
description: These Horses are among the most noble of the Endorian beasts. They were unknown as a breed before ca. T.A. 2545, the approximate date that the first breeding herd was fully established in Rohan by Eorl the Young; however, individual Mearas were to be found in Middle-earth before that date. They are commonly (and correctly) believed to be the descendants of Nahar, the Steed of Oromë, as are the Elven Horses. The progenitor of the breed in Rohan was Felaróf, who was white; such was his strength as an ancestor that most of his descendants are white or dappled gray. All Mearas, regardless of color, share Felaróf's other characteristics: they understand human speech, can be ridden without saddle or bridle (although they will tolerate such devices if needed), and live to a great age (eighty years is not unusual). Famous individuals include Felaróf (captured by Léod of the Éothéod, who named him Mansbane before Eorl renamed him), Shadowfax (Gandalf's steed, chief of the Mearas in Théoden's time), and Snowmane (Steed of King Théoden, who fell at the Pelennor Fields).

name: Kine Of Araw
description: The Kine of Araw are the largest of Middle-earth's bovids, that is creatures with split, even-toed hoofs. It is believed that these large, white cattle are the gift of the Vala Oromë (Araw). This is quite possibly true. The beasts are found on the shores of the Sea of Rhûn and on the Plain of Talath Harroch, east and south of Mirkwood. As far as anyone can tell, the Easterling tribes are the only people to have domesticated these creatures and used them as draft beasts, since the Rhovanion Northmen revered the Kine and used them only for breeding other cattle. Easterlings employed Kine to pull the Great Wains of their chiefs. During the brief existence of the Wainrider Kingdom in Wilderland (T.A. 1854-99), dozens of these magnificent beasts were pressed into service, and many perished in the raiding and wars that accompanied the Wain-easterlings' occupation. Many people of central Middle-earth value the long gray horns of these cattle, both to use as trumpets and drinking horns and to cut into sections for superb compound bows.

name: Stone-trolls
description: Trolls that turn to stone when exposed to sunlight, found in rocky areas and hills.

name: Red Wolves
description: Chief predator (S. Draucaran, Q. Carnanarmo) of the settled reaches between the Misty Mountains and the Blue Mountains, particularly common in southern Eriador from Cardolan south to the Belegaer. Unlike the Grey Wolf it is a vicious, tireless hunter prone to kill for sport, preying chiefly on sheep and unafraid of Men, readily attacking lone individuals or small groups. Sheep Hounds have proved an effective counter to it.

name: Cave-trolls
description: Trolls that dwell in caves and underground passages, adapted to darkness.

name: Ents
description: Ancient tree-like beings, shepherds of the forests, also known as Onodrim.

name: Lamprey
description: A cylindrical, grey, jawless fish, the Lamprey is a parasite that attaches itself to other creatures, like a leech with a circular maw. It then remains in place, slowly burrowing into the flesh and swallowing blood until sated. (A wound from a Lamprey will bleed at a rate of two hits/round for each round it was in place.)

name: Lassaraukar
description: Leaf-demons (Q. "Lassarauko"), nocturnal wood-spirits seduced and corrupted by Morgoth into savage arboreal predators. Immortal and unbreedable, with hearing and smell beyond Elven sight, they race along branches faster than a Swift flies and can stop instantly only at predetermined spots. Tall, featureless and greenish-hided, they live in "lats" of six (five Lesser led by one Greater) and rest motionless most of the day, often mistaken for statues (their eyes worth 500 gp).

name: Mabelmaikli
description: Sharp-claws (Q. "Mabelmaikli") are huge creatures with eight appendages. They resemble a cross between a Crab and a Squid and inhabit the inland seas of Rhûnen, Nûrnen, Dushera and the sheltered coastal shelves between the Bay of Helcar (in the Romenëar) and the Bay of Ormal (in the Haragaer). Because they are enamoured of rich, somewhat briny water, though, Mabelmaikli can thrive in any marsh, delta, lake, or ocean where there is a little salt. They are most common in Mordor and in the waters off Arg-Simorîg. Mabelmaikli feed on Starfish, Shellfish, and submarine Worms, but they will attack any animal that comes within reach of their four nimble, steely claws. Each of these pincers is 1-2 feet in diameter and can crush a Man with ease. Together, they present a frightening array. Actually, they are simply the creature's four forward, grasping appendages, and are used to immobilize prey. The Mabelmaikla tears its victims apart with nine inch mandibles and four pairs of mouth parts. Like their Crab relatives, Sharp-claws propel themselves backwards when they swim, but they move much faster, often at speeds of up to fifteen miles an hour. Generally, though, they prefer to burrow and remain inactive until their prey happens by. Their ability to lay an ambush is legend, especially among the slave-fishermen of Nûrn. After all, Mabelmaikli are huge, bright purple denizens that one would expect to see with relative ease. By using refined instincts and the cover of their burrows and murky waters, however, these beasts hide very well. Sharp-claws make excellent feeding machines. They 'hear' with sensors in their four legs and smell and feel with their three foot long antennae. Pain does not affect them. (They cannot be stunned.) Once a Mabelmaikla senses a prospective kill, it darts forward and embraces its victim with one or more pincers. Then it smells the prey with its feet, analyzing the pinioned creature's worth as food.

name: King Spiders
description: King Spiders are similar to but unlike the Giant Spiders of Mirkwood. They do not build large webs but rather dig deep pits, 2-3 ft wide and 8-10 ft deep. A King Spider caps this pit with a lid cleverly woven from a small amount of spider-silk, disguising the covering with leaves and other vegetation so that it is extremely hard (-30) to detect. Victims falling through the lid receive one to three +25 short sword attacks from the strong, knife-like silk tendrils. The pits are usually built on slopes. They support no more than 40 pounds of weight. The K i n g Spider, who has been waiting in a small side passage, leaps upon the victim and biles it, injecting its special poison, Kennesank. A RR f a i l u r e of 1-50 results in paralysis; a failure of 51+ results in death as the internal organs dissolve. If the victim dies, the spider will eat it. If not, the thrifty spider saves the meal. If it is a female who has recently mated, she may lay 6-12 eggs on top of the helpless prey and leave, sealing the hole more sturdily behind her. In two days, when the spiderlings hatch, they will spend three weeks living on the food left behind by their mother. Surely one of Endor's most vicious insects, the rare but often lethal Hummerhorn is the stuff of legend. Each weighs 6-8 lbs and has a razor sharp, pointed proboscis of chitin (transparent horn). It gores a victim to death, then feasts on the corpse with three sets of mandibles. They resemble gigantic black and grey wasps, and their only failing is the noise they make whenever they approach.

name: Jackals
description: Scavenging canines found in arid and desert regions, known for their cunning.

name: Daen folk
description: The indigenous pre-Númenórean people of the coastlands and plains, shorter and stockier than the Dúnedain, who adopted Westron and much of Númenórean culture while retaining their oral traditions and ballads; they form the majority of the common population of Lebennin and Anfalas.[file:73]

name: Daen Coentis
description: Mountain clans of the White Mountains living above the timberline in primitive groupings; semi-nomadic goat shepherds and fisherfolk on the coasts (Dunir of Andrast), they have largely avoided cultural contact with Gondor and still honor ancient Daen traditions.[file:73]

name: Galadhrim (Elves of Lórien)
description: The Silvan and Sindarin Elves of Lórien, known for their skill in woodcraft, stealth, and the construction of flets among the mallorn trees.

name: Sakalai
description: Small, dark-haired, independent tribes in the Loeg Balimur and remote Ethir Anduin, believed to be of mixed Daen and Southron stock; they live as swamp fishermen largely outside Gondorian authority.[file:73]

name: Elves of Rivendell
description: Rivendell is home to a mixed Elven population: Noldor lords and craftsmen, Sindar and numerous Silvan Elves who tend the grounds, agriculture and patrols.[file:244] Elves are tall, ageless, disease-immune, highly resistant to heat and cold, and in Rivendell they live in a hierarchical but willing social structure centred on Elrond as Lord of the House, with Silvan Elves often delighting in service and colourful dress, Sindar and Noldor taking leadership and craft roles, and nearly all residents trained to aid in defence when needed.[file:244]

name: Elrond
description: Elrond Half-elven, descended from both Eldar and Edain, founded Imladris in S.A. 1697 after the fall of Eregion and has since served as Lord of Rivendell, keeper of heirlooms of Arnor and wielder of Vilya, the Ring of Air.[file:244] His household includes his family (Celebrian, Elladan and Elrohir), other Noldorin lords, craftsmen, scholars and hosts, forming a cultured, self-sustaining enclave that also serves as headquarters for the Rangers and meeting place for the White Council in the late Third Age.[file:244]

name: Furry oak
description: Tall, closely spaced hardwoods with branchless lower trunks and umbrella crowns about forty feet up. Their dense canopies and thick bark make Southern Mirkwood's interior dark, damp and resistant to many pests and blights.

name: Chap-beech
description: Companion beech-like trees with smooth trunks and tight crowns that interweave with furry oaks. Together they form the shadowed, foggy forest typical of the southern eaves of Mirkwood.

name: Marsh Adders
description: The Marsh Adder is a large, aquatic serpent common throughout all marshy areas of central Endor. Especially prevalent in the Dead Marshes and Nindalf (the Wetwang), this creature is an excellent swimmer whose mottled gray and brown scales allow it to go all but unnoticed in the foul waters of the marshes. Marsh Adders are easily startled and bite with little provocation, usually seeking out small creatures such as ducklings and frogs but also attacking larger beings if threatened. Its large, curved fangs deliver its poison deeply. The (10th lvl) venom can be lethal: many victims (RR failure of 51+) die in five rounds due to respiratory paralysis, while a less imposing dose (RR failure 01-50) gives flaccid paralysis, leaving the joints limp and the victim without voluntary muscle control for 1-100 hours.

name: Morgai Flies
description: Vicious, biting, parasitic insects, Morgai Flies are one of the nasty nuisances of life in Mordor. They can found all over the Black Land, particularly in Gorgoroth, where they are known as Orcflies. Their sole food is blood, but enjoy feeding off nearly any beast they can find. Relatives of common Horseflies, Morgai Flies are large and dun, gray, or black in color, with red eyes and a red blotch, like Sauron's Lidless Eye, on their backs. Many chroniclers believe them to be monsters of the Dark Lord's design. This may be true of some of the larger subgroups, but those of the basic variety are simply huge, primeval vermin. Morgai Flies carry virtually all bloodborne diseases, and they played a major part in spreading the Great Plague of T.A. 1636-1637.

name: Grey Flyers
description: A slimy, silvery, gliding frog, the Grey Flyer is a small but deadly amphibian. They carry a nasty venom in their bluish claws. Grey Flyers are rare, living only in the depths of thick rain forests, but they live in huge colonies and woe to anyone who stumbles upon their nests!

name: Grape-leaf magnolia
description: A broadleaf evergreen with large, holly-like leaves and downward-hanging blood-red flowers. Its nectar yields a dark red honey that can be mildly intoxicating, prized by the Beijabar for brewing magnolia mead.

name: Mirkwood rose-tree
description: Tall, hedge-forming shrubs or small trees bearing masses of rose, burgundy and white blossoms from late spring to autumn. Needle-sharp thorns make them formidable living walls, first planted by Elves as boundaries and later valued by healers for their nectar.

name: Milk-white trumpet (Giant Datura)
description: A tall, foul-scented shrub up to twelve feet in height, with huge white trumpet-flowers that shed pollen explosively. The pollen can cause nausea and temporary blindness; the black seeds are potent psychoactive poisons—one intoxicates, several can be fatal to most races, though Elves are said to be immune.

name: Din Fainen (death-moss)
description: A pale, clinging moss that grows in dark, damp hollows and on rotting trunks in the shadowed forest. Contact or ingestion can be deadly; it is carefully harvested for use in the most virulent poisons.

name: Loth-nu-Fuin (Glorious Lichen)
description: A striking lichen that forms pale, intricate patterns on rocks and old trunks in the deepest shadowed parts of Mirkwood. It is used by lore-masters and alchemists as a marker of deep shadow and in rare draughts tied to dreams and visions.

name: Lebethron
description: Lebethron is a dark, fine-grained hardwood of Gondor, valued for its strength and smooth finish. It is used for walking-sticks, spear-shafts, carved fittings and select ship and house timbers.

name: Athelas (Kingsfoil)
description: Athelas, or Kingsfoil, is a slender-leaved healing herb found along old ways, in sheltered dells and near springs. To most it is only a pleasantly scented plant used in simples and poultices, but in the hands of the rightful kings and those with great healing power its leaves, bruised in hot water, can recall the wounded from the shadow.

name: Gwingyrn (Elven ship-timber)
description: Gwingyrn is a tall, straight, pale-barked tree prized by Elven and Dúnedain shipwrights. Its wood is light but strong and holds fastenings well, making it ideal for masts, spars and planking.

name: Oiolair of Tolfalas
description: The Oiolair is an evergreen coastal tree associated with the isle of Tolfalas and the seaward fringes of Gondor. With glossy leaves and aromatic resin, it weathers salt winds and storms, binding cliffs and dunes with its roots.

name: Mallos
description: Mallos is a fair flower of Gondor, bearing soft golden trumpets on slender stems. It grows in sunny meadows and sheltered coastal lawns, often mingled with alfirin.

name: Alfirin
description: Alfirin is a delicate white or pale flower of Gondor, often described as star-like or bell-shaped. It blossoms in quiet meads and along gentle slopes, frequently found together with mallos.

name: Beech
description: Beeches are smooth-barked hardwoods with dense, high canopies and a carpet of leaf-litter beneath. In southern Gondor they linger in upland groves and ravines, marking older, cooler forest pockets amid cleared farmland.

name: Date palms
description: Date palms grow along the warm southern coasts of Gondor and in sheltered river-mouth oases. In Lamedon and the Ethir Anduin their tall, feather-fronded crowns mark irrigated gardens and estate orchards, providing sweet fruit, shade and fibre.

name: Ancient mountain pines of Rhudaur
description: These ancient mountain pines cling to the steep valleys and hillsides of Rhudaur and the upper Anduin basin. Tall, dark-needled and deeply rooted, they endure cold winds, thin soils and heavy snow.

name: Goblin-gate cave fungi
description: Goblin-gate cave fungi are a collection of pallid mushrooms, molds and lichen-like growths that thrive in the damp, lightless tunnels under the High Pass. They feed on rotting timbers, refuse and the leavings of Goblin feasts.

name: Medicinal herbs of the Anduin Vales
description: This term covers the many healing herbs found in the Anduin Vales: simples that ease fever, restore strength, close wounds or clear the lungs. Woodmen, Beornings and wandering healers know where to find such plants along stream-banks, in forest glades and on sunlit hills.

name: Wild persimmons
description: Wild persimmon trees growing in warm, sheltered vales and river-terraces of Southern Rhovanion. Their orange fruit ripens late, offering sweet, sticky food for Beijabar, Woodmen and wildlife alike.

name: Carefree mustard
description: A hardy yellow-flowering herb of fields and river-banks whose seeds and leaves are used in poultices and teas to ease fevers, clear the chest and restore appetite. Common in the Anduin vales and Talath Harroch farmsteads.

name: Galadriel
description: Lady of Lórien, a Noldorin princess and Ring-bearer, whose wisdom and foresight shape the realm's defenses and long-term stance against Sauron.

name: Splayfoot goodwort
description: A low, broad-leafed herb of damp meadows and marshy margins. Infusions are said to steady the nerves, cheer the heart and give courage before battle or long journeys, making it a favorite of Northmen healers.

name: Birch
description: Birches are light-barked, fast-colonising trees that favor cooler slopes and disturbed ground. Their white trunks and fluttering leaves brighten Lamedon's higher vales, often forming mixed groves with oak and beech on the edges of older forests.

name: Elm
description: Elms are tall, broad-crowned trees common in moist river-vales such as the Anduin Vales. Their arching branches form shaded avenues along banks and field-edges, and their deep roots help bind alluvial soils.

name: Dunlendings
description: Also called "Hillmen", this group of Common Men traditionally lives in the hills and highlands west of the Anduin valley. Most have become settled farmers and herders and make up majority populations in Eriador. Others remain pure and live in the foothills and mountain vales. Physical Character: They are of medium to stocky build and rugged, with little body hair. Men average 175 pounds, women 135 pounds. They have a tanned or ruddy complexion and brown hair. They are superb mountaineers and handle tough terrain with little additional effort. Men average 5'10", women 5'6". Lifespan: Short; about 50-80 years. +20 for Climbing and Acrobatic maneuvers. +20 for Public Speaking and Acting activity. Crude wool and hide tunics with leggings. They also wear rough over-coats, short cloaks, and fur hats. Dunlendings are herders, hunters, and gatherers who live in extended families and reside in villages. Dunlendings generally hate the Rohirrim and Woses above all, although many also despise the Dúnedain, Dwarves, and Orcs.

name: Hillmen of Rhudaur
description: Short, dark, stocky men of the rugged hills of Rhudaur, distantly related to Dunlendings; superb mountaineers but deeply suspicious of outsiders, prone to treachery and ambush. Worship vengeful petty deities with blood sacrifice. Traditional enemies of both Dúnedain and Northmen; greatly fear Elves. Some served in the hosts of the Witch-king of Angmar alongside Orcs, an uneasy alliance. Survive as hunters, gatherers and occasional brigands in the broken terrain.[file:88]

name: Calenardhons
description: The Calenardhons are Men inhabiting the plains south of Fangorn (precursors or analogues to the Rohirrim). The 'Borders' chapter (6.11) describes their settlements along the Entwash and their interactions with Fangorn, including trading, grazing near the forest and occasional conflicts or alliances with Ents and border powers.[file:154]

name: Asdriags
description: A savage, wain-riding Easterling people who wander the grasslands in search of plunder.

name: Oak
description: Oaks are broad-crowned hardwood trees common in the temperate forests of Middle-earth. In Lamedon they survive as remnants of once greater woods, providing strong timber, acorns for wildlife and shade for farms and roads.

name: Crakes And Coots
description: A black-and-purple ducklike species, the Crake is known as a noisy and obnoxious waterbird. It has wide, splayed feet, allowing it to dash across pondweed and mudflats. Crakes are also excellent swimmers and remarkably swift flyers. They are among the most flexible and adaptable species in all of Middle-earth, ranging as far north as Forodwaith, Mur, and Urb. The Crake closest relative, the Coot, builds island nests up to three or four feet across from reeds and weeds.

name: The Necromancer of Dol Guldur
description: Sauron in his guise as the Necromancer, occupying Dol Guldur since the 12th century T.A., radiating fear and corruption, breeding Orcs, trolls, spiders and fell beasts, and acting as the dark center of Southern Mirkwood.

name: Rural Men
description: This is a generalized label for the rural folk of Eriador and Gondor, the Common Men of northwestern Middle-earth. These folk contain varying degrees of Harnadan, Dunlending, Northman, and Dúnadan blood. Physical Character: All types, but normally medium. Males average 160 pounds, women 125 pounds. Variations of fair to tan or olive skin. All colors of hair and eyes. Average endurance. Males average 5'10", women 5'4". Lifespan: Moderate, averaging 60-80 years. Practical wool and linen garb, including cloaks, boots, etc. Tunics are worn in warmer areas; shirts and pants or leggings are worn in cooler locales. Rural folk are mostly farmers and herders, with little contact with areas outside their village or region. Rural folk are suspicious of most outsiders, and might be in awe of some. They hate no race, aside from the Orcs and Wargs.

name: Woodmen
description: The Woodmen are Northmen who have long lived in Mirkwood, the great forest in Rhovanion. They are a loose tribe of hunter/gatherers who live in or below the trees as extended families, bands, or clans. Physical Character: Average to stocky, with strong but angular features and large amounts of facial hair. Fair skin with reddish highlights. They have blond hair and blue or green eyes. Average endurance. Men average 6'1", women 5'5". Lifespan: 45-85 years. They can climb and move along tree limbs exceedingly well: +20 to Climbing and Acrobatics maneuvers. +10 to Foraging maneuvers. They wear crude woolen tunics and short pants with leggings. Most favor coats, cloaks, and fur hats. They are an independent lot who have no formal political structure. Living off the gifts of the forest, they reside in small, close groups secluded from other races. They interact with few other than the Beornings and Silvan Elves. Woodmen hate Orcs, Wargs, Trolls, and Giant Spiders.

name: Beornings
description: Also called Beijabar or Bajaegahar, this dispersed group of large men has a confused origin. They are a Northmen branch related to the Woodmen, Lake-men, and Dale-men of Rhovanion, although they apparently became distinct in elder times, probably before the Northman migrations out of Eriador. Physical Character: Physically, the Beornings are the largest of the Northmen, and all are strong of build and bone structure. Men are exceedingly hairy; they average 220 pounds. Women average 145 pounds. Their complexions are fair, but unlike most Northmen (who are usually blond), the majority have reddish hair. Beornings need sleep only twice every three days. Men average 6'4", women 5'7". Lifespan: 80-100 years. Wild animals will not attack a Beorning unless actively provoked. They are extremely hairy, and their tendency to wear furs gives them an "animal-like" appearance. Beornings have traditionally lived in small groups or single families on well-tended "manors". Some, like Beorn's line, have favored relative lowlands, while the majority reside in the passes and foothills of the Misty Mountains or Grey Mountains. Beornings hate Orcs, Wargs, Trolls, Giants, and Dragons.

name: Northmen
description: Descendants of Vidugavia's horse-lords in the plains and East Bight, formerly prosperous farmers and horse-breeders, reduced by the Plague to hard-edged defenders of isolated homesteads and fortified camps.

name: Eothraim
description: Superb equestrian warriors, semi-nomadic herders, and hunters who move between seasonal pastures and maintain a disunified but highly honorable society.

name: Radagast the Brown
description: The Wizard dwelling at Rhosgobel on the western edge of Mirkwood, devoting his power and attention to birds and beasts rather than politics, and providing a rare point of light and refuge in the shadowed woods.

name: Small Reptiles
description: The lesser lizards, skinks and snakes that scuttle through warm rock, scrub and ruin. Harmless to all but insects and each other, they are signs of dry, sun-warmed country.

name: Gondorian farmers
description: The founding of Gondor concentrated people in the Anduin valley, settling Anórien, Ithilien and Lebennin together as early heartlands.[file:216] By the War of the Ring, Anórien's lowlands host scattered villages and farmsteads of Gondorians of mixed Númenórean and local descent, who work fields and pastures and supply traffic along the Great West Road and river.

name: Gramuz
description: Brave, resilient foresters and horse-herders with strong warrior traditions.

name: Elven Horses
description: The Horses of the Elves, like the Mearas, are directly descended from Nahar, the steed of Oromë. Nahar is immortal; his descendants are not, but Elvish Horses live to great age (100 years is not unusual). They rarely have white markings except for an occasional white "star"; large white patches (painted/piebald/skewbald) are never seen, though all other colors of lesser Horses occur, with white and silvery dappled gray more common. They prefer to live in wooded areas, as the Elves do, rather than the plains home to most Horses. More intelligent than most Horses, they need no bit or bridle, being empathetic enough to receive directions from their riders; most Elves do without a saddle. Finally, Elven Horses stand staunchly in the face of dangers that would terrify most Horses, brave even before supernatural terrors such as Trolls and the Undead. Famous individuals include Nahar (Steed of Oromë), Nimros (Steed of Glorfindel), and Rochallor (Steed of Fingolfin).

name: Sagath
description: Nomadic horsemen and chariot-riders who frequent the pastures.

name: Thranduil's Silvan elves
description: Silvan Elves under Thranduil living in caverns and halls near the eastern eaves of the forest, lovers of feasting, song and languages and largely indifferent to neighboring Men and Dwarves unless threatened.

name: Dwarves of the Iron Hills
description: Durin's Folk who mine and smelt rich iron deposits in the Iron Hills east of Mirkwood, providing metalwork, weapons and armor and maintaining trade links with Dalemen and Lake-men.

name: Variags
description: The Variags live in the region of Khand, a semi-arid plateau which lies southeast of Mordor. They are a distinct race, but are occasionally confused with the Haradrim of Far Harad. In reality, they have as many ties to the Easterlings. Brutal and semi-nomadic, they have long been influenced by Mordor and the constant wars with their neighbors. Physical Character: Medium; men average 160 pounds, women 125 pounds. Extremely dark grey or black skin, straight black hair, and red or reddish brown eyes. Variags can travel for extreme periods on horseback with little or no rest. Medium; men average 5'9", women 5'3". Lifespan: Short, about 50-70 years. Variags are superb riders and handle both horses and camels well. +10 bonus to Offensive Bonuses when fighting from horseback (+5 bonus when fighting from camelback). Variags favor black and red clothing and wear richly adorned garb. Their armor is designed around hideous, frightening beast designs. They carry gold or gilded weapons. Always at war, Variags live a brutal and exciting life. Most are herders and raid the stocks of their enemies.

name: Wandering Eothraim
description: Semi-nomadic herders and horse-masters who clash with the Easterling tribes over pasture lands.

name: Rohirrim Messengers
description: Anórien's Great West Road is heavily used by Gondorian messengers and troops, and during the War of the Ring the Rohirrim ride through its northern strip on their way to Minas Tirith; Tolkien explicitly describes the beacon fires behind Anórien and the riders answering them.[web:234][web:235] The region's population therefore includes transient military columns and supply trains on top of the resident peasantry.

name: Urban Northmen
description: Merchants and settlers from the Nan Celduin valley, speaking Rhovanion and Westron, who came to share in eastern trade and now inhabit fortified towns, culturally close to Gondor but proud of their Northman identity.[file:37]

name: Bandits and deserters
description: Disaffected soldiers, plague-maddened peasants and local rogues haunting the roads and ruins, turning the depopulated frontier into a patchwork of robber bands unconcerned with Gondor or Sauron beyond what they can steal.[file:37]

name: Black Bears
description: Strongly built but compact bear (Q. "Meglivorn"), common throughout northern Endor. Nocturnal omnivores that gorge before winter hibernation; superb sense of smell but weak hearing/eyesight. Solitary, the female travelling with 1-4 cubs. Eriadoran Northmen, the Lossoth and the Beijabar/Beornings hold near-cultic bear traditions.

name: Sea lords of Lebennin
description: High Men of Gondor, especially the Sea-lords whose power is rooted in Pelargir and the coastal towns; they command fleets, administer the province and lead the struggle against Corsairs and Haradan privateers.[file:30]

name: Gondorian Quarrymen
description: Gondorian laborers who occasionally work the ancient limestone quarries (like Nardol Quarry) on the forest's northern margins.

name: Dwarves of Erebor
description: A community of Durin's Folk dwelling in the Lonely Mountain, a great isolated peak north of Long Lake, using its caverns as halls, mines and treasure vaults; tightly connected to trade along the River Running.

name: Marshmen
description: Small, long-lived descendants of the Edain dwelling in Nindalf and western marshes, living by hunting, fishing, goat herding and gardening, worshipping Eru (Iroi) and the Valar as Jevinn and Eremi, and organizing themselves through yearly councils on the holy island of Tel Belarin; some have been driven westward by the Dead Marshes' corruption.[file:37]

name: Eraguk
description: Fierce steppe nomads of mixed Haruze and Nórniag lineage, the Eraguk roamed the Aegardh since ancient times as the original inhabitants; after centuries of Gondorian pressure, most were driven east or assimilated, surviving in small, often bandit-style groups by T.A. 1640.[file:73]

name: Haruze
description: Haruze farmers and nomads who have occupied the Vale of Harnen, growing sorghum and herding flocks, moving seasonally between Harad/Nurn and the southern Harondor plateau; a persistent cultural presence even when Gondor claims political control.[file:73]

name: Gondorian rangers
description: Gondor maintains border fortresses like Amon Eithel and patrols the Hyarmenti road to keep the Aegardh nominally under control; these rangers are thin on the ground and frequently harassed by nomads, Haruze and Umbarean agents.[file:73]

name: Umbarean agents
description: After the Kin-strife, Umbarean and Haruze captains encouraged nomadic clans to migrate into the Aegardh, using Harondor as a staging ground against Gondor and the Hyarmenti trade road.[file:73]

name: Tharbad people
description: Merchants, craftsmen, guildsmen, smugglers and refugees crowd the fen‑ringed city of Tharbad, whose power shifts from Cardolan's kings to Gondor's Canotar and then to powerful guilds as war, flood and plague erode royal authority.[file:31]

name: Rangers of Ithilien
description: Gondorian rangers who launch stealth raids against Sauron's forces.

name: Cave Worms
description: Wingless, breathless earth-element Drakes of the Cave-drake family that dwell deep underground in cavern systems.

name: Princes of Dol Amroth
description: Hereditary Dúnedain rulers of Dor‑en‑Ernil, holding a status just below the King and Steward; their line carries Elven blood and rules almost autonomously from Dol Amroth, symbolized by a silver Swan‑ship on deep blue.

name: Eredrim
description: Dunlending‑related highlanders of the Ered Tarthonion, descendants of the Daen Coentis; rugged village dwellers ruled by their own chieftains, paying tribute in kind, practicing animistic and sometimes dark rites and resisting assimilation.

name: Elves of Edhellond
description: A Sindar-led Elf-haven at the mouth of the Morthond, famed for its white swan-ships and as guardians of Eryn Laegol; the Sindar provide shipwrights, captains and leaders while Silvan Elves form most of the common folk, hunters and fishers. Closely allied with the Princes of Belfalas, they build the most beautiful ships in Southern Gondor.

name: Celeborn
description: Lord of Lórien and consort of Galadriel, providing military and diplomatic leadership and embodying Sindarin traditions within the Golden Wood.

name: Grass Cats
description: Handsome, long-legged hunter of the plains of Calenardhon, now quite rare since Men have killed or domesticated most of its prey and hunt it for fear of losing sheep, goats or kine. Its thick buff fur absorbs grass pollen in summer, turning an eerie yellow-green. It hunts by leaping onto prey's back and driving its claws into throat and neck, and has killed Men this way; in heat or combat it gives off terrifying high-pitched shrieks on winter nights.

name: Orcs of Khazad-dum
description: Sauron's servants who plunder the ancient dwarven vaults.

name: Stoors of New Maresh
description: A small Stoor Hobbit community living in burrows on hills rising from a marsh near the Dunstrem, traveling by pole‑boats, trading herbs and fish, and protected by the treacherous wetlands and their own wariness of outsiders.[file:32]

name: Merchants
description: Merchants based in Larach Duhnnan and other sites move goods between clans and to Tharbad, while a hidden Smugglers' Hold grows gort and tartella herbs for Corsairs of Umbar using Dunnish and Dúnadan frontmen.[file:32]

name: Ice orcs
description: Tall, gaunt, pale, sun-tolerant Orcs descended from Morgoth's northern guards, ruling the Forodwaith from the Dark-Ice Fortress and mining outposts like Thollakar; they raid neighboring Lotan and Lossoth, worship Morgoth in gruesome festivals, and now answer uneasily to the Witch-king of Angmar.[file:38]

name: Gondorian settlers
description: Gondorians of Calenardhron and Dor Rhûnen who garrison forts like Thorontir and farm pockets such as Tharlorien and the Undeeps, guarding the fords and asserting Gondor's claimed dominion.

name: Silvan Elves (Wood-elves)
description: When the Eldar departed from the original Elven homeland during the Elder Days, a number of their brethren remained behind. They decided not to seek the light of the Aman and were labeled as the Avari (Q. "Unwilling, Refusers"). These kindreds were left to fend for themselves during the days when Morgoth's Shadow swept over the East. During these dark times they were forced into the secluded safety of the forests of eastern Middle-earth, where they wandered and hid from the wild Men who dominated most of the lands. They became known as the Silvan or Wood-elves. Physical Character: Most are slight of build, and all are thin by mannish standards. Ruddy of complexion, with sandy hair and blue or green eyes. Generalizations are difficult, however, since there are many kindreds and there is wide variation among them. Same endurance as for Noldor Elves. Males average 6'0", females 5'9". Same lifespan as for Noldor Elves. Cannot become sick or scarred and are virtually immune to disease. -10 bonus versus cold attacks. Their vision is the same as that of Noldor Elves. The Silvan folk are superb musicians and have tremendous hearing, even for Elves. +10 to Bardic spell attacks. They are also elusive. +10 for trickery and Stalking/Hiding maneuvers. The culture of the Silvan Elves is best characterized as unstructured and rustic by Elven standards, but rich and relatively advanced when compared to the ways of Men. They have always been independent, but as of late many have settled in kingdoms ruled by the Noldor or Sindar. They are met as the common folk of the woodland realms — hunters, foresters and bowmen of Mirkwood, Lorien and the eastern eaves — wary of strangers and seldom straying far from the trees.

name: Slirdu
description: Strange arboreal creatures (the Slird, pl. "Slirdu") found only in the forests of the remote islands off the southeast coast of Middle-earth, most in colonies of 2-40 on the isle of Vulm Shryac. Green or green-with-red-stripes, they are four- or five-armed invertebrates resembling both squids and starfish, adapted to land with true lungs and a quasi-skeleton of thick insulated nerves; each prehensile arm joins a squid-like trunk holding two eyes, a beak-like mouth and an ink sack. Nesting near treetops and moving fast through the canopy, these true carnivores eat ~20 lbs of meat a day, enveloping prey, immobilising it with suction pads and spraying toxic ink that stuns, blinds or knocks out; their rudimentary nerve-stem brain makes them little more than honed killing organisms.

name: Common Orcs
description: These hideous creatures are members of a race descended from Elves who were twisted and perverted by Morgoth during the First Age. Although they are not inherently evil, they are culturally and mentally predisposed toward Darkness. Physical Character: Heavy, with thick hides, short legs, and long, thin arms. They have grotesque, fanged faces and random hair growth. Black or grey hair, black or reddish-brown eyes, and deep grey or black hides. Tremendous endurance. Ores can travel for 2 days without rest. Alternatively, they can run for up to 12 hours without stopping. 3-5 feet. Indefinite lifespan; certainly hundreds of years, but the nature of their warlike life permits few to live past the age of 50. +30 bonus versus heat/fire attacks. Orcs sleep during daylight hours, although they need rest only once every 3 days. In most darkness Orcs can see like Elves, and in absolute darkness they can see 10'. Bred as laborers and warriors, Orcs respect power and terror above all things. They join and cooperate in substantial groups only when led by a "focused will", some overwhelmingly strong individual. Across the lands they are met in different masters' service: as Sauron's soldiery in Mordor and Dol Guldur, as the goblins of the Misty Mountains and Gundabad under their own chiefs, and as the war-bands of Angmar and the cold North.

name: Turtles
description: Freshwater and marsh turtles of river, pond and fen, slow on land but at home in the water. Most are harmless grazers and scavengers, though the larger snapping kinds can take a finger or a duckling.

name: Dwarves of Durin
description: Durin's heirs and their people who, after the loss of Moria, established mining colonies at Silverplunge, Thunderclift, Silverpit, Long Peak and Norr-dum, and later reconnected with Erebor; ruled by a king advised by Dwarf-lords and by appointed Wardens in outlying colonies.[file:38]

name: Smugglers
description: Semi-legal privateers, outright pirates and smugglers operate from coves, swamps and hidden harbors along the coasts and the Ethir Anduin, raiding trade and dodging royal duties despite the Sea-lords' efforts.[file:30]

name: Dúnedain
description: The Dúnedain are descendants of the Edain who settled on the island kingdom of Númenor during the Second Age. It was their Númenorean forefathers who colonized and conquered much of Middle-earth. When the Isle of the West sank into the sea during the Downfall of S.A. 3319, two groups survived: the Black Númenoreans and the Faithful Dúnedain. The latter settled Arnor and Gondor, the Realms in Exile. Like all their brethren, they have traces of Elven and Maia blood. Physical Character: Tall and strong. Men average 225 pounds, women 150 pounds. Fair skin and black or dark brown hair. They have grey, hazel, blue, or green eyes. Considerable endurance. Men average 6'5", women 5'10". Lifespan: 100-300 years. Rich and well made, with elaborate collars. The Dúnedain of lowland Gondor wear light tunics and often go without leggings. Those of colder areas wear more. They favor feathers and white symbols on dark fields. Comfortable and centered around cosmopolitan cities and towns. The Dúnedain hate the Corsairs, for they blame them for the Kin-strife, Gondor's civil war. They also despise the Black Númenoreans.

name: Hurndaen
description: Pukel-creatures that look like iron pillars until disturbed, when they become deadly iron manlike forms; like other Pukel they can reconstitute themselves, though this takes at least 50 years of undisturbed repose.

name: Sand Drakes
description: Sand Drakes are one of the two breeds of Were-worm, burrowing Dragons of arid and desert lands.

name: Mountain Giants
description: Extremely long-lived, reclusive beings of immense size who dwell in the high peaks and avoid contact with other races.

name: Corsairs
description: The Corsairs are descendants of the Dúnedain rebels who fled from Gondor in the wake of the Kin-strife of T.A. 1432-47. Most settled in Umbar in T.A. 1447 and began a long struggle for control with the stronger Haradan and Black Númenorean elements. Since that time modest inbreeding with the locals has created a somewhat distinct group, although their Dúnedain roots remain strong. They long to reclaim Gondor as their own. Physical Character: Strong of build; males average 220 pounds, women 145 pounds. Corsairs are generally fair skinned and have grey or bluish eyes and black or dark brown hair. Considerable endurance. They do not get seasick. Men average 6'3", women 5'9". Lifespan: 95-190 years. +25 bonus for Weather-watching or Star-gazing activity. Corsairs favor purple, red, and gold clothing made from silk or fine cotton. They wear tunics and only rarely don leggings. Men wear gold collars, while women are well-adorned with jewelry. Corsair culture reflects the conservative elements found among Gondor's aristocracy, only it is carried to an extreme.

name: Dwarf Miners of Harlindon
description: Dwarves operating coal and iron mines on the eastern slopes of the southern Blue Mountains.

name: Swamp Stars
description: Ghosts that have wholly degenerated into small bright lights which lure the unwary (5th-level Luring) into bogs, quicksand or deep marsh pools, draining life-energy as the victim drowns or is immobilised.

name: Snakes
description: Wild snakes of grass, rock and water, most shy and harmless but some venomous. They bask on warm stones and slip away from heavy feet, striking only when cornered or trodden upon.

name: Dwarves of Nan-i-Naugrim
description: Firebeards and Broadbeams of the Blue Mountains, living in the fourteen holds of Nan-i-Naugrim; they maintain active smithing traditions and weapon-crafting at Merlost.

name: Elves of Mithlond
description: High Elves of Cirdan's haven who keep watch over the Elostirion tower and the western road, and crew the grey ships — the Sunset-rounders that bear the Eldar from the Grey Havens into the Undying West.

name: Rock Vipers
description: An inconspicuous little creature, the Rock Viper would not be worth mentioning, save that those climbing cliffs and mountains in Endor would be well-advised to watch where they place their hands or their bare feet (Hobbits, especially), for this snakeling is fond of sunbathing on rocky ledges. Their grey skin is speckled with brown and black spots, enabling the serpent to blend in with rock surfaces (the creature is also called the Leopard Viper). Although it is small, the Rock Viper's bite is quite deadly (20th lvl). There has never been a proven case of anyone surviving such a bite without some sort of medical treatment, either magical or herbal. The victim first suffers swelling, massive bruising, and discoloration around the wound. Then, within 60 seconds, his heart begins to palpitate, his breathing becomes irregular, his endocrine system fails, and he dies of total system shock within 6-10 minutes.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "Central Misty Mountains", "probability": "Very Hard"}, {"region": "North Misty Mountains", "probability": "Very Hard"}]

name: Highland Lynxes
description: Hunting cats of the less extreme foothills and mountains throughout Endor, smaller than the Snow Leopard but effective hunters and fishers. Unusually for cats their size, they are highly social, living in prides of 5-8 with an intricate structure and hunting large game as a group using wolf-like tactics. Taken young, they make loyal and protective pets.

name: Men of Rhudaur
description: Men of the former kingdom of Rhudaur; many soldiers, especially land-hungry peasants, enlist in Angmar's armies and later settle as soldier-farmers in fortified villages.

name: Estaravi
description: Northmen of the Great River valleys; some young men, especially those with strong warrior values, join the Witch-king's forces seeking wealth and opportunity.

name: Angmar veterans
description: Ex-soldiers of Angmar settled as freemen in fortified villages along the borders; they farm, patrol daily, escort supply trains, and provide additional troops in wartime.

name: Hillmen shepherds
description: Hardy nomadic herders who graze their sheep in the southern valleys.

name: Angmarean Patrols
description: Orc scouts and Mannish soldiers operating from border towers like Cargash.

name: Rangers of the North
description: Dúnedain rangers monitoring the movements of the Witch-king's armies.

name: Rangers of Arthadan
description: Frontier shepherds, small steads and watch-forts on the northwestern marches of Arthedain use the fringes of Talath Muil for grazing and as a staging ground, but the bleak climate and exposure prevent large-scale settlement. During the wars with Angmar, local militias are pinned on frontiers and armies cross these uplands, as described in the accounts of battles around Fornost and the Emyn Uial.[file:179][file:132]

name: Faithful of Arnor
description: For a time the Rammas, like the lower but more abrupt down-lands to the south, serves as a religious retreat for the Faithful of Arnor. Monastic houses, hermitages and small religious enclaves occupy hilltops and defiles, combining contemplative life with subsistence gardening and herding in the sheltered pockets of the ridge.

name: Lumimiehet
description: The Lumënyihmënyt roam the northernmost stretches of the Rammas Forod from early spring through mid-autumn. Their camps, perched on rocky slopes, are crude hunting sites intended to last days or at most weeks. Reserved and wary, they usually break camp and move rather than deal with rough strangers, but if approached carefully they can provide detailed information about local geography, hazards and notable features.

name: Tinkers and travellers
description: Tinkers and peddlers move from steading to steading along hardpacked and paved roads, doing odd jobs, selling goods and carrying news and gossip. They are described as friendly and talkative, equal in information value to merchants and often more approachable, shaping the social fabric of the hills.[file:179]

name: Tom Bombadil
description: The oldest being, who sings songs to calm the angry trees of the forest.

name: Rivermen traders
description: River-folk who ply the Baranduin and other northern rivers, claiming the waters as their own and serving trade and communication between Arthedain, Lindon and other regions.[file:25]

name: Beffraen
description: A relatively primitive folk akin to Drúedain, inhabiting the dark woods of the Rast Vorn peninsula; they are secretive, use the treacherous forest and coasts to remain largely outside Dúnadan control and maintain their own customs.[file:31]

name: Grass Grouses
description: A plump, gallinaceous (chickenlike) bird of the grasslands of Calenardhon (Rohan), the tasty Grass Grouse is the preferred prey of many hunters, be they winged or two- or four-legged. Many a man, Red Fox, or Hawk will pass easier prey by for a crack at a greasy Grass Grouse. Their black-speckled brown plumage provides good camouflage in the tall grasses, so the birds prosper and spread in spite of their inherent appeal to the palate. The male booming mating cry is most reminiscent of a drunk blowing over the neck of a bottle.

name: Saralainn
description: Rowdy, semi‑independent clans of Saralainn and the Gwathló mouthlands whose culture blends Dunnish traditions and refugee influences, often providing both raiders and hardy defenders in the south.[file:31]

name: Rohirrim
description: The Rohirrim (S. "Masters of Horses") are the Northmen of Rohan and have a complex ancestry. They settled in Rohan (then Calenardhon) about T.A. 2510 at the request of Cirion, the Steward of Gondor. The grant was a reward for the Northmen's aid in the defeat of the Easterling Balchoth. The Rohirrim call Rohan the Riddermark. Physical Character: Average to stocky and strong. Men average 190 pounds, women 130 pounds. They have considerable amounts of facial hair. Blond, with fair skin and blue eyes. Average endurance, although they can ride as long as their mounts can endure, and as long as they can stay awake. Men average 6'1", women 5'5". Lifespan: 60-85 years. +20 bonus to melee Offensive Bonus when fighting from horseback. Herders and horse-masters, they spend much of their year living in various semi-permanent camps set out on a circuit of pastures. They return to their permanent homes for the winter. Some garden or farm. Most Rohirrim are also accomplished hunters and fishermen. The Rohirrim hate the Dunlendings and consider the Woses as lesser beings. They also have an age-old hatred of Orcs, Wargs, Easterlings, and Dragons.

name: Hounds
description: In Middle-earth the term Hound (Q. Huo, pl. Huron) almost always means the fierce Wolfhound, ranging from the diminutive dogs of Hobbit farmers near the Shire to the great noble beasts the Men of old used to hunt Wargs and the werewolves of Morgoth. Found wherever Men or Hobbits dwell, they sometimes run wild in deserted lands. The most famous was Huan of Valinor, one of the immortal hounds of Orome, who followed Celegorm into exile, aided Luthien and Beren, and both slew and was slain by Carcharoth.

name: Snow-elves (Lossidil)
description: The Lossidil Snow-elves maintain Helloth, an enchanted ice city on the Landless Land, and patrol the polar wastes and Talath Uichel's far north, guarding Helecthil's radiance and intervening occasionally in mortal affairs.[file:39]

name: Iathrim and Laegrim
description: Sindar (Iathrim) and Green-elves (Laegrim) occupy Harlindon and southern Lindon, many as survivors of drowned Beleriand. Some resist living under Noldorin rule and eventually leave eastward to join Silvan realms, but others remain and shape Lindon's woodland culture and rituals described in the culture and lifestyle sections.[file:99]

name: Lindellim (Nandor)
description: Nandor Elves (Lindellim) and Mithrim-related northern Sindar houses contribute additional Elven lineages within Lindon. The appendices detail house histories, territories and military forces, showing how they occupy various parts of Forlindon, Harlindon and Rhûnlindon and how their demographic share grows relative to the Noldor over time.[file:99]

name: Angmarim Soldiers
description: Mannish soldiery of Angmar — hardened northern fighters in the service of the Witch-king, garrisoning his black towers and marching against the Dúnedain of the North. Grim and disciplined, they are the Iron Crown's mortal sword-arm.

name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
description: Dwarves inhabit halls and mines on the east side of the Blue Mountains, especially south of the Gulf of Lhûn; Thorin's Halls (Durin's Folk in exile) are located in this Lindon-adjacent zone. The introduction notes that even after all Elves depart in later Ages, Dwarves still dwell in their halls and keep mines in use.[file:99]

name: Goblin King of Gundabad
description: Title for the tyrannical overlord who rules Mount Gundabad and claims suzerainty over all Orc tribes of the mountains; his court in the spires is rife with intrigue, assassination, and revolt.

name: Priests of Darkness
description: Orcish priests who preserve records of goblin deeds over two Ages and guard secret lore and rituals in hidden chambers, including tales of the coming from Morgoth's realm.

name: Helechoth
description: A land-focused Ystvät Talven sub-culture relying heavily on herd management and inland hunting, occupying central Talath Uichel and interacting with both Lossoth and Angmar's frontier forces.[file:39]

name: Umli (Half-dwarves)
description: The Umli live in the Far North of north-central Middle-earth, east of the Lossoth. They are a race of short folk who apparently came from an ancient union of Men and Dwarves. Legends call them Half-dwarves. Physical Character: Strong and compact, with large features and considerable facial hair. Males average 150 pounds; females 135 pounds. Ruddy skin and red hair, with penetrating pale blue eyes. Exceptional endurance in cold climes; average in temperate areas; poor in warm or hot climates. 4-5 feet. Lifespan: 100-200 years. +30 bonus versus cold/ice attacks and a -15 bonus versus heat/fire attacks. Need sleep but three hours a day. In the cold (below 35°) they halve any encumbrance penalties; above 90° they suffer double encumbrance penalties. Hunters, gatherers, and fisherman, they move from site to site depending on the season. Most reside in caves or underground settlements. Many mine, and the Umli are accomplished smiths and artisans.

name: Great Eagles
description: The Great Eagles are the most noble of all birds, having been created by the Valar Manwe, Lord of the Air, and Yavanna, Queen of the Earth. Appointed as overseers of all the Kelvar, they have played many glorious roles in the history of Middle-earth. They have rescued numerous champions in their hour of greatest need: both the Noldo Elf Maedhros, imprisoned in Thangorodrim, and Gandalf the Grey, confined at Orthanc by Saruman, owe their salvation to these graceful creatures (they also aided Gandalf after his combat with the Balrog of Moria). Great Eagles are as intelligent as any of the Free Peoples but are not numbered among them, presumably because they lack the power to build things and fashion crafts. These huge birds are not to be trifled with: fierce foes when aroused to anger, with little patience for faint-heartedness in the face of danger. They are stalwart foes of Evil and especially hate any other creature of the air who has allied with the Valar enemies.

name: Noldor (High Elves)
description: The Noldor are often called "High Elves" or "Deep Elves", ostensibly because they are considered to be the most noble of the Quendi in Middle-earth. In reality, they are so named because they are the only Elves living in Endor who have ever resided in the Blessed Realm of Aman across the sea. This exalted status is accentuated by their close ties with the Valar, a relationship which accounts for their unique cultural and linguistic roots. Other names for the Noldor include Deep-elves, Golodhrim or Gelydh (Sindarin labels), and Nómin (Edain label). Physical Character: Of all the Elves, the Noldor are the strongest and sturdiest of build, although they are still slimmer than Men. Most are dark haired and have greyish eyes which betray a proud looking manner. Descendants of the Fingolfin and Finarfin are often fair haired and blue eyed, for their blood contains Vanyar influences. They do not carry great burdens, but they are capable of traveling 16-20 hours a day. The Noldor do not sleep; instead they rest in a trance for 1-3 hours a day. Males average 6'7", females 6'3". Immortal and will only die due to violence or if they weary of life and lose the will to live. Cannot become sick or scarred and are virtually immune to disease. +20 bonus versus cold attacks. Unparalleled at understanding and working with crafts. +20 for Item Use rolls. Across Endor they are met in different guises: as lords, smiths and loremasters in Lindon, Rivendell and the ruined craft-halls of Eregion, and as wary wandering companies, scouts or exiles in the wild lands between.

name: Dwarves of Khazad-dum
description: Durin's Folk in their ancestral seat of Khazad-dum (Moria); allied Dwarves who trade extensively with the Noldor of Eregion, exchanging ores and craft for Elven goods and knowledge, a friendship driven by shared interest in craft and learning.

name: Celebrimbor
description: Master Smith, founder of the Gwaith-i-Mírdain and de facto leader of the craft community; arguably the second-greatest Elven smith after Fëanor, and the maker of the Three Rings.

name: Daen Iontis
description: Militant Dunnish faction exemplified by clan Arailt and their allies, bitterly hating Dúnedain, closely linked to the Temple of Justice, and pushing for war and a harsh, sacrificial religion that twists old Daen beliefs.[file:32]

name: Vault Shrikes
description: The Vault Shrike is a tiny, bright red Bat with a long nose and tongue with which it pokes into holes and crevices for insects to eat. It also secretes from its tongue a highly toxic nerve poison that enables it to deal with larger prey. These Vault Shrikes paralyze or kill by means of the poison. It then rips its victims to shreds with its tiny sharp teeth. The Vault Shrike diet is further enhanced by its ability to catch small frogs in an ironically froglike manner: by snatching them in midleap with that long tongue. Vault Shrikes get their name from their unnerving, shrieking cry. They are only found in Moria and the caverns of the Misty Mountains.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "Moria", "probability": "Very Hard"}, {"region": "Central Misty Mountains", "probability": "Very Hard"}, {"region": "North Misty Mountains", "probability": "Very Hard"}]

name: Merënyetsënyt
description: Ystvät Talven who dwell on moving icebergs and along the Bay of Forochel's shores, hunting whales, seals and walruses; they are stocky, short, fair-haired and remarkably resistant to cold and immersion, but suffer high mortality from the hazards of the sea.[file:39]

name: Noldorin mystics
description: An obscure order of Noldorin mystics maintains Evermist, a hidden valley on the Cape of Forochel that serves as a haven for the Free Peoples and base for resistance to the Witch-king of Angmar.[file:39]

name: Olog-hai (Black Trolls)
description: The Olog-hai have been bred by Sauron from lesser Troll stock, and have until late been a rare breed. Cunning and organized - yet as big and strong as their lesser brethren - the Olog-hai are superb Warriors. They know no fear, and thirst for blood and victory. Olog-hai are also called Black Trolls, for they have black scaly hides and black blood. Most carry blank shields and war hammers, although they are adept at using almost any weapon. They differ from older Troll varieties in other ways as well: (1) they ignore bleeding or stun results when given a critical strike from a normal weapon, since they are more resistant to unenchanted weapons; (2) they can operate freely in daylight; (3) they are relatively quick; and (4) they are capable of using normal language properly, and speak Black Speech (Rank 5) and Westron (Rank 3).

name: Half-trolls
description: Half-trolls are the product of a union of Olog-hai and Variag Men. For this reason, they are sometimes confused with Black Trolls, but they are smaller and quicker and vaguely resemble Men. Their 7' height, jet-black skin, long sharp red tongues, and glowing red eyes should also serve notice of their uniqueness. Half-trolls differ from Olog-hai in other ways: (1) they wear crude black clothing and considerable amounts of armor; (2) they are kept away from other Trolls; (3) they are as intelligent as Men; and (4) they are more agile. Given their increased minds, they get two background points.

name: Half-elves
description: Half-elves are a rare breed, the offspring of Men and Elves. While the Dúnedain, Corsairs, and Black Númenoreans have Elvish (and even Maia) blood, their connection to Elves lies in the remote past and they are not called Half-elves. True Half-elves have one Elven parent and one Mannish parent. Upon reaching adulthood, a Half-elf must decide whether to live as a mortal Man or an immortal Elf. Elrond of Rivendell chose the latter course, while his brother Elros - first King of Númenor - opted for mortality. Physical Character: Strong and slender, thinner than Men but stouter than Elves. They have thin, angular features. Males average 200 pounds, females 140 pounds. Fair, generally with brown or black hair and grey eyes. Considerable endurance. Half-elves need but 4 hours rest a day: sleep for mortals or meditation for the immortals. Males average 6'3", females 5'11". Mortal Half-elves live 250-500 years. Virtually immune to disease and natural illness. +5 bonus against cold attacks. Can see extremely well in the dark. Under moonlight or starlight they can see perfectly for 500'.

name: Echo Hawks
description: The Echo Hawk is a large subterranean bird and a menace that few would expect to meet underground in a cave. It is a raptor that has totally adapted to life in a lightless environment, utilizing both infravision and echo-location for its hunting. The echo Hawk nostrils are unusually large, giving it a keenness of scent quite rare in a bird. Its beak and talons are large, hard, and sharp, so much so that the Echo Hawk can pierce through most types of armor. As is usual with the raptors, the female Hawk is larger than the male. Otherwise the sexes are similar, with dull white feathers, colorless skin, and beaks and talons like translucent horn.

name: Flying Foxes
description: A variety of large bat, the Flying Fox makes its home amid brambles or in the tops of dense, partially-submerged trees of the coastal wetlands. They are most common in the marshes and scrublands of the East, although a few smaller varieties make their homes in Rhun and one group lives in southern Eriador. Unlike most bats, the Flying Fox thrives in daylight, having good eyesight and a refined sense of smell. Using its long snout, it hunts rodents in the scrub and bites wild berries which might elude its more flatfaced cousins. The Flying Fox is considered synonymous with evil magic in many areas of eastern Middle-earth.

name: Locusts
description: Grasshopper-like insects that breed in dry seasons into vast devouring swarms, stripping field and pasture bare in hours. A locust-year spells famine, and the darkening cloud of their flight is dreaded by every farmer.

name: Great Falcons Of Mirkwood
description: Rarely seen despite their huge size, Great Falcons reside in the remoter and higher reaches of the Forest of Mirkwood (actually most reside in the Emyn-nu-Fuin, the "Mountains of Mirkwood"). They are almost untameable, probably due to the difficulty of hooding and confining such a huge bird, even as an eyas (fledgling). Great Falcons are surprisingly maneuverable for such large birds, as is necessary for any winged beast that would live in the dense forest. Their light build makes them unable to carry more than around 70 lbs, so they make poor mounts.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "Woodland Realm", "probability": "Sheer Folly"}, {"region": "Mirkwood Wilds", "probability": "Sheer Folly"}, {"region": "Dor Guldur", "probability": "Sheer Folly"}]

name: Red Eagles
description: The Red Eagle dominates the skies of the East. Smaller relatives of the Great Eagles, who are rare in eastern Endor, these magnificent predators are relatively numerous and pose a threat to travellers in the Red Mountains and Mountains of the Wind. Fortunately, though, they usually prefer to hunt goats, sheep, or wild horses. Red Eagles are known for the deep red hue that accents the tips of their gold-colored plumage. They have scarlet beaks.

name: Chamber Bird
description: White, mute, and almost blind, the odd, fungus-eating Chamber Bird is one of the many strange inhabitants of Moria. They seek out the fungus by their remarkable sense of smell (odd indeed for a bird) and note enemies by their inaudibly high cries, in the manner of bats. Their large, gaping beaks have serrated edges and their bluish talons are both sharp and strong. Totally herbivorous (primarily fungivorous), they rarely attack animals unless surprised or starving (some instinct for meat seems to remain from some predacious ancestor).

name: Hunting Bats
description: A large, carnivorous bat of the White Mountains, Hunting Bats are attracted to the smell of blood. They will pounce upon a bleeding victim, tearing flesh from bone with their small, razor-sharp teeth. In addition, they are notorious carriers of disease. Hunting Bats usually dwell near the entrance of a cave (on the inside, of course), but they can also be found in the crevices of cliffs and in abandoned buildings.

name: Losandamundar
description: The rare white, long-haired Losandamunda (Q. "Snow Elephant") of the Far North is related to the Andamunda of southern Endor. These furry behemoths, though, are rarely domesticated. Instead, they are worshipped (notably by the Uax, but also by the Myri and Angcla tribes) or hunted (notably by the eastern Lossoth and Lotan).

name: Eris Squids
description: This small tentacled sea creature is found along the Mumakani coast, in the warm South Sea. It is hunted by alchemists and others who prepare magical substances for its ink. The exact use of this solution in magical preparations is not known to those outside this isolated southern region of Middle-earth. Mumakani and Kiran peoples, however, prize the substance for its ability to produce camouflage paste and invisible ink.

name: Sindar (Grey-elves)
description: The Sindar or "Grey-elves" are Eldar and were originally part of the great kindred called the Teleri. Unlike the Noldor, Vanyar, and bulk of the Teleri, the Sindar chose not to cross over the sea to Aman; instead they stayed in Middle-earth. They, like the Silvan Elves, are part of the Moriquendi, those Elves who never saw the Light of Valinor. Physical Character: Thin when compared to Men, the Sindar are nearly as tall as the Noldor but are generally slighter of build. They are more muscular than the Silvan folk. Most have fair hair and pale blue or grey eyes. Like the Noldor, they have light skin. Same endurance as Noldor Elves. Males average 6'5", females 6'1". Same lifespan as Noldor Elves. Cannot become sick or scarred and are virtually immune to disease. +15 bonus versus cold attacks. Vision same as that of Noldor Elves. Less musical than the Vanyar or Silvan Elves, and are less skilled in forging or crafts than the Noldor. Nonetheless, they are adept at all the arts and excel in their special provinces. No race builds better boats or ships. The Sindar are the most open and cooperative of Middle-earth's Elves. They are great teachers and borrowers and have an interest in the works of all races. Scattered through the eastern woods and the realms of their Silvan kin, they are encountered as counsellors, march-wardens and lords among the Wood-elves of Mirkwood, Lorien and the Vale of Anduin.

name: Horses Of Mordor
description: The forces of Evil, most notably the corrupted Maia Sauron, have bent Horses to their nefarious purposes. Sauron originally had his Nazgûl mounted on black Horses stolen as foals and thus accustomed to evil at an early age; these beasts were fortified by spells and potions to make them swift, tireless, and unafraid of things that would terrify a normal steed. By the end of the Third Age the Dark Lord had succeeded in reshaping some of the stolen foals: enlarged and strengthened, given carnivorous foreteeth, fiery night-sighted eyes, and steaming, smoking breath, their faces became skull-like and their hooves like polished iron. One such was the Mount of the Mouth of Sauron, which bore its master to the parley at the Black Gate during the War of the Ring; exceedingly large and powerful, it could run almost as fast as a Meara, with sharp-edged hooves and saw-like jaws that penetrated armor. Its greatest weapon was its foul steam, akin to the Nazgûl's Black Breath (a 30-foot range, affecting any target within a 5-foot radius). Note: this creature can be characterized as a monster; see Section 6.0 regarding Evil Horses.

name: Pond Bats
description: A small black creature, the Pond Bat rarely attacks Men or Elves unless panicked and confused, or trapped. It is, however, a noted carrier of fleas and other parasites, which it leaves in isolated dwellings and camps. Thus, they are a common cause of Cyclic Fever.

name: Jatewoones
description: The Jatewoone is also known as the cleaverbeak or marsh bird. Standing 9 feet tall and endowed with Swan-like necks and long, powerful legs, these white waterfowl sport a scarlet band across the eyes. From a distance they might look like a large crane, although they are not of that family. Their beak is a light grey with the exception of the sharp, brilliant indigo dorsal ridge from which they get their name. Birds of prey in every sense, their legs end in talons that can grip even small creatures tightly, and their beaks are made for tearing. Jatewoones live on snakes, fish, and small mammals. Their reputation for hostility comes from their protection of their nests, usually hidden in swampy areas; they will also attack when surprised by noisy people, and can split a skull. They mate for life and produce one or two eggs every year or every other year; the young grow at a remarkable pace. Jatewoones can fly, but if the need to escape danger arises, it is more common to see them running at high speeds, using stillwater areas for cover.

name: Great Green Pheasants
description: The Great Green Pheasant is a handsome bird. Females and their immature offspring sport lustrous bronze-green plumage. Emerald green feathers and long, trailing tail plumes adorn the male. The cocks are famous for their ferocious duels over potential mates: they rush at each other fiercely, broadsiding their rivals in clouds of emerald green down, until one or the other retreats, exhausted and bedraggled. Great Green Pheasants, like the Grass Grouse, are considered tasty, but this larger bird must be hung before eating, as it is tougher (in every way) than the passive Grass Grouse.

name: Gorcrows
description: Smaller cousins of the evil Crebain, Gorcrows are not so much evil as indifferent to suffering. Not nearly as bright as their larger cousins, Gorcrows live to eat (although, if asked, they would probably insist that they merely eat to live). They are attracted to shiny things, and Gorcrows have picked up unbelievable numbers of shiny trinkets and small coins. Their untidy, communal nesting sites are often littered with minor yet still significant amounts of wealth. However, since Gorcrows will cooperate in protecting their nest sites, would-be plunderers are well-advised to be very cautious and to wear helmets with small eyeslits: Gorcrows are notoriously fond of eyeballs!

name: Great Bats
description: Seldom-seen, the grey and purple Great Bat is found in remoter parts of Mirkwood. Little is known of its habits. It eats night-flying birds (e.g., Owls), large spiders, and various rodents, although larger individuals have been known to fell a small bear. The Great Bat sucks blood (always a tempting notion to those who dislike bats, even the small insectivorous kind) from its victims, and feeds on the softer flesh of its prey.

name: Ravens
description: Ravens are among the more intelligent birds to be found in Middle-earth. Not as noble nor as wise as the Great Eagles, Ravens are nevertheless on the side of good, unlike all too many of their lesser Corvidae cousins. They are reputedly the messengers of the Vala Namo (Mandos) and are associated with Fate and Prescience. The Ravens of Erebor befriended and counselled the Dwarves of the Line of Durin during the Third Age. These noble birds live a long time: Roac of Erebor lived past 150 years of age. Brilliant and somewhat enchanted, most Ravens can speak the tongues of the Free Peoples (or at least of Men and Elves; the tongue of the Dwarves is too secret and that of the Ents too lengthy for ravens to learn). Conjurers have long sought out the foreknowledge of Ravens.
