name: Fallohides (Forest Hobbits)
description: The Fallohides are the tallest and average between 3 and 4 feet in height. They have fair skin and, of all the groups, resemble Men the most. Their numbers are small, however, and some observers have confused them with nearby mannish groups. Fallohides enjoy the woodlands found in cool northern climes. Some have begun relying on surface dwellings of wood, brick, or stone. Fallohides are the most prone to mingle with Big People. Harfoots and Fallohides trace their lines through either the male or female. Those Hobbits who dare venture away from home are assumed to be extraordinary, and therefore get five background points.

name: Black Númenoreans
description: The term "Black Númenorean" (S. "Mornúmenedain") is used to describe Men of Dúnedain stock who are descended from the "Unfaithful" Númenorean colonists and conquerers who came to Middle-earth during the middle and late Second Age. These Unfaithful had broken with the Valar and Elves who had bequeathed them the island kingdom of Númenor at the beginning of the Second Age, and many worshipped their own idols or paid homage to the Dark Lord Sauron. Physical Character: Strong and imposing, with angular features. Men average 225 pounds, women 150 pounds. After long years in the hot South, the Black Númenoreans have tanned, grey-brown skin, and black hair. Their piercing eyes are various shades of grey. Considerable endurance. Men average 6'4", women 5'10". Lifespan: 90-175 years. +25 to Sailing maneuvers. +10 to melee Offensive Bonuses, -10 to Defensive Bonus. Black and gold are their favored colors. They wear rich clothing made of dyed silk and cotton. Their societies are well-ordered and ruled by force of personality, backed by harsh law. Dictators and oligarchs hold sway over the people.

name: Easterlings
description: This term refers to a collection of peoples who live in north-central Middle-earth, the area to the east of the western shore of the Sea of Rhûn. These nomadic confederations are ever in search of territory and wealth and periodically invade the lands of their neighbors. The Balchoth and Wainriders are two such peoples. Each group has its own subculture and language, although most are related. Physical Character: Compact and agile. Men average 140 pounds, women 100 pounds. Swarthy, with a tan or yellowish/tan quality. Normal endurance, although they can ride horses, wagons, or chariots for long periods without exhaustion. Short to medium. Men average 5'6", women 5'1". Lifespan: Short, about 40-65 years. They are superb with riding animals and produce fabulous horsemen. +10 bonus to Offensive Bonuses when fighting from a horse or chariot. +20 to any attempt to train, befriend, or subdue a horse. They are nomads who live in mobile camps and move their camps using great wagons (wains). Warriors all, they also herd horses and cattle. Easterlings have little regard for anyone except their own people. They hate the Dúnedain.

name: Half-orcs
description: Half-orcs are a hideous creation, born of Man and Orc. They are often confused with Uruk-hai, but are a distinct race, small in number but capable and deadly. Their origin is also unclear, although it appears that they were first used by the tainted Wizard Saruman. He still employs them as agents, spies, lieutenants, and special guards. They are particularly effective in Eriador, for Saruman's Half-orcs have Dunlending blood in them and are capable of blending into their societies. Half-orcs are akin to Uruk-hai, but look more like dark Men. They speak Westron quite well (Rank 5), and most know both Orkish (Rank 3) and a little Black Speech (Rank 2). Half-orcs have no restrictions on professions, and get three background points.

name: Uruk-hai
description: Following Sauron's resurrection in T.A. 1000 he began breeding a new race of Orcs, one capable of independent and intelligent action. After centuries of work, he produced the first Greater Orcs, and he called them the Uruk-hai (BS "Orc-race") because they were more suited to the formation of societies. Initially, the Uruk-hai remained close to Sauron and served as lieutenants and elite guards, but gradually their numbers grew and their strain strengthened. Their existence remained guarded until Sauron was willing to show his hand. Nonetheless, in T.A. 2475 they were unleashed in full scale battle formations. They are the Dark Lord's finest goblin troops. The Uruk-hai differ from normal or Lesser Orcs in many ways: (1) they stand 5-6 feet tall; (2) they have a more "human" appearance, despite cat-like eyes, fang-teeth, and black/grey hides; (3) they have longer, stronger, and straighter legs; (4) they are intelligent and cunning; and (5) despite their preference for darkness, they operate freely in daylight. They speak Black Speech (Rank 5), Orkish (Rank 4), and good Westron (Rank 4).

name: Haradrim
description: This is a collective name for the peoples who occupy the great arid and semi-arid lands south of Harondor and Mordor, the land called Harad. Since this is rough and unblessed territory, their greatest populations lie beside the seacoast, rivers, and bays; however, Harad contains little true desert and sparse groups settle or roam the whole region. Haradrim (S. "Southmen") are also called "Southrons" or "Haradwaith" (a term also referring to the land). Physical Character: North and central desert - Most groups are small and wiry, particularly those of the open country: males average 130 pounds, women 100 pounds. Coastal areas and Far Harad- Most are related to the Men of Mûmakan, and are tall and wiry: males average 180 pounds, women 140 pounds. Northern groups have light/medium grey or brown skin, straight black hair, and dark brown eyes. Southern groups have dark grey, brown, or black skin; straight or curly black hair; and dark brown or black eyes. +10 bonus versus heat/fire attacks; -10 bonus versus cold/ice attacks. Their eyes are attuned to extremely bright light and they are unaffected by brilliant displays which might blind others.

name: Slaves
description: Captives and subjugated people from various backgrounds who work the land to support the warrior elite, although the harsh climate forces some warriors to continue agricultural labor.

name: Woses
description: Of all Men, none surpass the Woses in the arts of wood-lore and wood-craft. This very ancient and diminutive race has long been tied to the forests and has remained the greatest lot of woodland warriors ever produced by the Second-born. Their skills have in fact guarded their narrow survival, for they are considered ugly by Men and Elves alike and have been hunted and persecuted since the days of the First Age. The Woses have many names. What they call themselves is not altogether clear, although the name Drûgs is taken from their own tongue. Drúedain (sing. Drúadan) is the label given them by the Elves, while Men call them by various labels: Rógin (sing. "Róg"), Púkelmen, Wild Men, or Woses, the latter a Westron term. Orcs fear the Woses, and have named them Oghor-hai. Physical Character: Woses generally have a broad, stumpy profile marked by wide faces, flat features, and deep-set eyes. Short, thick legs, heavy lower bodies, and pronounced brows help give them an eerie character. Ruddy skin, black hair, and black eyes. 4 to 5 feet. Lifespan: 30-50 years. The wide noses of the Woses are the most sensitive found in Man. Even in an open field, they can smell an Orc before another Man can see him. +25 bonus to Tracking maneuvers; +15 bonus for Foraging. They have the ability to sit for days on end without movement. Woses are blessed with superb night-vision. Even in the dark forest of the night they can see 1000 feet as if it were daylight.

name: Mithrim Noldor Patrol
description: A roving war-band of Noldor of Mithrim descent that keeps the old marches of the Blue Mountains and northern Lindon. They watch the passes of the Ered Luin and the wooded vales below for Orcs out of the ruined North, and escort travellers between the scattered Elven settlements.

name: Lossoth
description: The Lossoth live in the Far North of western Middle-earth and are often called "Snowmen" or "Forodwaith" (the latter is also a name for their land). They are a sparsely settled, nomadic folk who move with the seasonal migrations of big game and rarely interact with other Men. Physical Character: Stocky and hard, with pronounced muscles. They have wide features and large, pudgy hands and feet. Men average 175 pounds, women 140 pounds. Fair, with reddish highlights. They have pale blue eyes and fair hair. They are extremely rugged and can travel great distances with little or no rest. Short; men average 5'5", women 5'3". Lifespan: Medium, about 75-90 years. +20 bonus versus cold/ice attacks. -20 bonus versus heat/fire attacks. They have an acute sense of smell and perception. They can pick up a Man's scent a mile downwind and 100 feet upwind (1000 feet otherwise). +10 bonus to Perception. The Lossoth are poor, nomadic hunters and gatherers. They use stonework, bone, and limited amounts of wood and metal (mostly copper). Some herd reindeer and all make use of hunting dogs. They hate Wargs, Dragons, Giants, and Trolls above all things.

name: Falathrim (Sea-elves)
description: Sea-elves who dwell by the coasts, skilled in shipbuilding and sailing. Along the shores of Lindon and Harlindon they appear as fishers and net-menders in the river-mouths, as shipwrights and sea-captains in the havens, and as the crews of the grey ships that keep the western coasts.

name: Urban Men
description: This is a generalized label for the town and city folk of Eriador and Gondor, the Common Men of northwestern Middle-earth. It includes common folk from Annúminas, Fornost, Bree, Tharbad, Calembel, Dol Amroth, Linhir, Pelargir, Osgiliath, Minas Tirith, etc. These folk contain varying degrees of Harnadan, Dunlending, Northman, and Dúnadan blood. Physical Character: All types, but normally medium. Males average 160 pounds, women 125 pounds. Variations of fair to tan or olive skin. All colors of hair and eyes. Average endurance. Males average 5'10", women 5'4". Lifespan: Moderate, averaging 65-85 years. Various types of elaborate or practical wool and linen garb, including cloaks, boots, etc. Tunics are worn in warmer areas; shirts and pants or leggings are worn in cooler locales. Some imported silks and fine cottons are in evidence. Urban folk are a varied, rather cosmopolitan lot. Many are from mercantile or guild backgrounds and some have dealt with enchantments. Urban folk are worldly enough to deal with most outsiders. They hate no race, aside from the Orcs and Wargs.

name: Bree-landers
description: Mixed folk of Bree and its environs, including men of various stocks and hobbits, living in and around a trenched, hedged village at the junction of the Greenway and Great East Road, once a vibrant trade-town but fading by T.A. 1640.[file:25]

name: Dorwinrim
description: The Dorwinrim occupy the lower Carnen valley and the region running south from the river to the northwest shore of the Sea of Rhûn - the land called Dorwinion. They are a mixture of Northman and Easterling stock. Physical Character: Dorwinrim are slightly larger than most groups labeled as "Common Men": males average 160 pounds, females 110 pounds. Overall, they are somewhat stocky and have wide features; narrow noses and eye slits are the only exception. Their fair, slightly yellowish complexion is unique. They have subdued eyes which are usually brown (like their straight hair), although some are blue or green. Average endurance. Men average 5'9", women 5'2". Lifespan: 60-80 years. +30 bonus to RR versus poisons. +15 for Acting or Public Speaking situations. +10 for Rowing maneuvers. They are traders and river-men who abandoned their nomadic past in favor of an urban, albeit rustic life. Dorwinion is known for its fine wines and strong oils. Vines cover the land and provide the principal sources of income: grapes, olives, wine, olive oil, etc.

name: Common Men
description: A broad label for the Common Men of Eriador and Gondor: the rural and urban folk of mixed stock who make up the bulk of the ordinary population, including farmers, herders, townsfolk, traders and the rootless roles of road and frontier. They are not a single lineage but the everyday people among whom the older Edain, Dunedain and Dunnish bloodlines have long mingled.

name: Avalanche
description: A sudden slide of snow or rock down a steep slope, triggered by thaw, storm or a careless noise, that can bury a party in moments. The high passes are most treacherous in spring and after heavy snow.

name: Asdriag & Sagath Soldiers
description: Warriors of the Asdriag and Sagath, Easterling tribes of the lands beyond Rhûn who serve Sauron's banners as auxiliaries. Horse-borne and lightly armed, they fight for plunder and the promises of dark lords, and trouble the eastern bounds of the free realms.

name: Crocodiles
description: Large water-lurking reptiles of the warm southern rivers, marshes and shores, lying like logs until prey comes near. Powerful and armoured, a big one can drag down a deer, a horse or an unwary man.

name: Beijabar of Nan Anduin
description: The Beijabar, a Northman folk of the Vales of Anduin akin to the Beornings — bee-keepers, herders and woodsmen who hold the riverlands and fight to keep them clear of Orcs and wolves. Independent, and hospitable to friends but fierce to foes.

name: Calenardhon Ruins
description: The ruins of Calenardhon, the grassland province of Gondor before it became Rohan — abandoned forts, road-stations and steadings standing empty on the plains, slowly reclaimed by grass and weather.

name: Broadbeam Dwarf Patrol
description: A patrol of Broadbeam Dwarves warding the roads and mine-approaches of the Blue Mountains. Grim and heavily armed, they guard the holds of Nan-i-Naugrim against Orcs and trespassers and keep the old ways open between the Dwarf-towns.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "Ardolyalinya", "probability": "Very Hard"}, {"region": "Ardquetto", "probability": "Very Hard"}, {"region": "Celanoriand", "probability": "Medium"}, {"region": "Ered Luin", "probability": "Medium"}, {"region": "Ered Luin Vales", "probability": "Medium"}, {"region": "Tol Fuin", "probability": "Medium"}]

name: Black Lizards
description: Dark-scaled lizards of rocky, broken country, basking by day and sheltering in crevices. Most are harmless hunters of insects, though larger kinds will take small prey.

name: Bramble
description: Dense, thorny bramble and briar that chokes wood-edge, ruin and waste — a tangle that tears clothes and flesh and slows any passage. In some old or tainted places the briars grow rank and clutching, almost as if with a will of their own.

name: Calenardhons Soldiers
description: Soldiers of Calenardhon, the great grassland province of Gondor before it was granted to the Rohirrim — garrison troops and militia holding its forts, fords and roads against Orcs and Dunlending raiders out of the hills.

name: Cathfiar
description: A fierce wild cat of the northern wilds, larger and bolder than a common wildcat, hunting alone through forest and fell. Quick and savage when cornered, it preys on hares, fowl and young deer.

name: Dunedain Traveller
description: A lone Dúnadan upon the road — one of the long-lived, far-wandering Men of Westernesse, abroad on some errand of their own. Grave, courteous and watchful, such a traveller makes a doughty companion and keeps their lineage and purpose close.

name: Dunlending Site
description: A site of the Dunlendings — a hill-fort, clan-hall, standing stone or burial cairn of the old folk of Dunland. Wary of strangers, the clans guard their places jealously and resent trespass.

name: Chasm
description: A deep cleft, crevasse or sinkhole — sometimes plain, sometimes hidden under brush or thin crust — into which the unwary may plunge. Old quarries, karst country and glaciers are riddled with such pitfalls.

name: Cliff
description: A sheer drop or precipice where a single misstep means a fatal fall. Cliffs bar the way along coasts, gorges and mountain paths, and a crumbling edge or sudden gust can send the careless over.

name: Cold Drake
description: Cold-drakes (Q. "Helkaloki") are the most common and oldest breed of Dragon, from which all others descend; wingless or winged, they haunt northern mountain caverns and are the Grey Dragons (Sintanguleki) of the Ered Mithrin.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "Forovirian", "probability": "Sheer Folly"}, {"region": "Iron Hills", "probability": "Sheer Folly"}, {"region": "Ered Luin", "probability": "Sheer Folly"}, {"region": "Dale", "probability": "Sheer Folly"}]

name: Dumbledoors
description: Great droning bumble-bees of meadow and heath, furred and harmless, blundering from flower to flower. An old country name for them is 'dumbledors', and their deep hum is one of the sounds of a warm summer's day.

name: Dragons
description: Dragons and drakes of Middle-earth — the great worms bred by Morgoth in the Elder Days and their lingering descendants. Vast, cunning and malevolent, armoured in scale and (the greatest) gifted with fire and a spellbinding voice; any one is a peril to a whole realm.

name: Druedain Site
description: A place of the Drúedain (Woses) — a carved Púkel-stone, hidden camp or forest shrine, marked by their watchful guardian-statues. Easily overlooked and best left undisturbed by outsiders.

name: Dunedain Site
description: A site of the Dúnedain — a ranger-shelter, boundary-stone, cache or ruined steading of the Men of Westernesse. Plainly made and well-hidden, such places often still serve those who know their secrets.

name: Falathrim Fisher Sailor
description: Falathrim of the Lindon coasts met about their daily craft — casting nets in the river-mouths, mending boats on the strand, or working a small smack along the Gulf of Lhun. Weathered and unhurried, they know every current and tide of Harlindon's shores.

name: Dunlendings Bards
description: Singers and lore-keepers of the Dunlending clans, carrying the genealogies, feuds and grievances of their people in memorized verse. Welcome at any hearth, a bard's praise or mockery can make or unmake a man's standing among the clans.

name: Dunlendings Healers
description: Wise-women and herb-lore keepers of the Dunlending clans, tending wounds and sickness with simples, charms and old craft. Respected and a little feared among their own folk, they guard remedies and rites handed down since before Rohan came.

name: Dunlendings Priests
description: Keepers of the old Dunlending faith — leaders of rite and sacrifice who tend the clans' ancestral spirits at hill-shrines and standing stones. They hold great sway over their people and deep suspicion of outland gods and ways.

name: Dunlendings Warriors
description: Fierce clan-warriors of Dunland, armed with spear, axe and round shield, who nurse an old hatred of Rohan for the lands their forefathers lost. They fight in loose bands under their headmen and raid the Westfold when the chance comes.

name: Dwarf Prospector
description: A lone Dwarf or small party searching the roots of the Blue Mountains for fresh seams of iron, coal or precious metal. Secretive about their finds, prospectors range the high valleys with pick, lamp and a keen eye for the lie of the stone.

name: Foreigners
description: Men from distant lands, marked by strange dress, speech and custom — traders, envoys, mercenaries or wanderers far from home, regarded with curiosity or suspicion depending on where their road has brought them.

name: Dyrians
description: Folk of Dyr in the eastern marches near Dorwinion and the Sea of Rhûn — traders, herders and rivermen living between the great realms, prizing their wine, horses and hard-won independence.

name: Eagles
description: Great hunting eagles of mountain and moor, sharp-eyed takers of hare, fowl and lamb. Larger and prouder than other raptors, they nest among high crags and are seldom troubled by any beast of the lowlands.

name: Farmer Woodsman
description: Settled country folk who work a farm or a stretch of woodland at the edge of the wild — tilling, herding, felling and charcoal-burning. Hospitable but guarded, they are the first to suffer when raiders or beasts come out of the wilderness.

name: Elk
description: Large deer of the northern forests and marshes, the bulls bearing broad spreading antlers. Powerful and aggressive in the rut, an elk (or moose) can be a match for a wolf or a careless hunter.

description: A ruin of the ancient Edain or their early kingdoms — older and ruder than the works of Gondor and Arnor, half-sunk in earth and forest. Such places hold old bones, older lore, and sometimes things best left buried.

description: The perils of fire-mountains and hot ground — lava, ash, choking fumes, scalding steam-vents and bubbling mud-pots. Found chiefly about Orodruin and other live peaks, where the very earth is treacherous.

name: Elven Site
description: A place of the Elves — a hidden refuge, garden, white tower or carved fountain, often beautiful and lightly guarded by enchantment. Time and the world pass it by, and few mortals find it unbidden.

name: Great Elk
description: An immense elk of the northern wilds, far larger than the common kind, with a towering spread of antlers. Rare and magnificent, a great bull fears few beasts and can gore or trample any that corner it.

description: An enclosed yard, garden or homestead-close — a walled plot of orchard, herb-beds or pasture about a dwelling. A small island of order and plenty, and a place a traveller might beg food or shelter.

name: Lossoth Site
description: A camp or sacred place of the Lossoth, the Snowmen of Forochel — snow-houses, sled-caches and bone-marked shrines on the frozen coast. Suited to the bitter North and unwelcoming to outsiders.

name: Hobbits
description: A rare meeting with Hobbits beyond their usual haunts — small, soft-spoken folk easily overlooked, generally a family or two strayed from the settled lands of the Shire, Bree or Dunland. Shy of big folk and quick to slip from sight, they want little more than to be left to their meals and pipes.

name: Eels
description: Long, snakelike fish of muddy rivers, meres and estuaries, hunting by night and hiding in weed and silt by day. Slippery and hard to hold, the larger ones bite sharply and some salt-water kinds grow alarmingly big.

name: Fish
description: The fish of river, lake and sea — from small fry to great pike, salmon and sea-fish — a staple food for folk and beasts alike. Their abundance shapes where fishers settle, and the greater predators of the deep are not to be taken lightly.

name: Fisherfolk
description: Folk who live by the water — netting fish in river and lake, gathering shellfish and shrimp along the shores, and poling boats through the shallows. Weathered and close-knit, they keep to their own stretch of bank or coast and read the water better than any map.

name: Insect Swarm
description: A dense swarm of biting or stinging insects — flies, midges, gnats or worse — thick enough to torment beast and man, foul wounds and drive cattle to madness. The bane of marsh and still summer air.

name: Forest-trolls
description: Trolls that haunt the deep woods — Mirkwood's southern eaves, Fangorn's marches and the tangled forests of the wild lands. Mossy and bark-hided, they blend with the timber until they move, ambushing travellers who stray from the path and dragging them back to their lairs.

description: The winged fell beasts that roost in the Emyn Guldur near Dol Guldur — featherless, reptilian flyers bred or drawn to the Necromancer's service and later borne by the Nazgûl. Their shadow and reek bring terror before ever they strike.

name: Fire Drake
description: Fire-drakes (Q. "Uruloki") are the slender, fire-breathing winged Dragons descended from true Cold-drakes; the greatest Worms of the North (including Smaug) belong to this family and lair in the Grey Mountains and Lonely Mountain.

name: Glacier
description: A great river of ice, slow-moving and riddled with hidden crevasses, unstable séracs and meltwater channels. Crossing one is a peril in itself, and the bitter cold and glare add to the danger.

name: Giant Spiders
description: Web-spinning descendants of Ungoliant; the lesser kind haunt Mirkwood and other gloomy places, speaking Westron and Black Speech and cooperating to trap victims with paralysing then dissolving venom. Greater ones include Shelob, guardian of Cirith Ungol in Mordor, and Enna San Sarab of Dol Guldur.

description: Soldiers of a Gondorian garrison out on their rounds — keeping the walls, roads and watch-posts of a town or fortress, checking travellers and watching the borders. Disciplined men of the South Kingdom, courteous to the lawful and hard on the suspect.

name: Great Lizard
description: A large predatory lizard of the southern wilds, big enough to threaten dogs, livestock and children. Quick over short distances, it ambushes prey and defends its lair with snapping jaws and lashing tail.

name: Grazing Animals
description: Mixed herds of grazing beasts — deer, wild cattle, antelope and the like — moving together across plain and meadow. Their numbers and constant wariness make them hard to approach, and they draw predators wherever they gather.

name: Grass Fire
description: A wildfire racing across dry grassland or scrub, wind-driven and faster than a running horse. It can trap herds and travellers in the open, and the smoke alone can choke or blind.

name: Great Serpent
description: A huge serpent of the southern marshes and rivers, long enough to coil and crush a man or a large beast. Patient and hidden, it strikes from the water's edge and drags its prey under.

name: Greater Dragon
description: A dragon of the first rank — ancient, immense and terrible, often fire-breathing and wing-borne, with a guileful tongue and a hoard to match its age. Few remain, and the coming of one is a calamity for kingdoms, not merely travellers.

name: Guard Tower
description: A fortified tower set to watch a road, ford, border or pass — manned in good times, ruinous or ill-held in bad. From its top a long stretch of country can be watched, and travellers below are seldom unseen.

name: Hillmen
description: Rough hill-dwelling folk of the upland country — herders, hunters and raiders who answer to clan and feud rather than king. Hardy and clannish, wary of lowlanders and quick to take up arms, they hold the high valleys and moors that softer folk avoid.

name: Horsefolk Éored
description: A mounted troop of the Éothraim horse-folk — riders ranging the grass-plains in war or on patrol, swift and disciplined, deadly with spear and bow from the saddle. Whether a small scouting party or a full éored, they cover ground no footman can match.

name: Hill-trolls
description: Big, brutish Trolls of the highlands and broken hill-country, met among the crags of the Trollshaws, the Coldfells, the Ettenmoors and the Misty Mountains. Stronger and hardier than Men but dull-witted, they lair in caves and ruins and raid lonely roads by night — and the older breeds must beware the sun that turns them to stone.

name: Huorn
description: Huorns — trees grown strange and half-awake, between tree and Ent, that can move slowly and feel anger. They gather in the deep shadows of Fangorn and the old forests, and folk who stray among them when they are roused seldom return.

name: Ice Drake Gondring
description: Gondring, a great cold-drake of the northern mountains — a wingless dragon whose breath is killing frost rather than fire. It lairs among high snows and frozen caves, and its passing leaves men and beasts rimed and dead.

name: Lesser Dragon
description: A lesser drake — smaller and less mighty than the great dragons, often wingless or fireless, yet still huge, armoured and deadly. Such worms lair in mountain, marsh and waste, raiding herds and folk and hoarding what plunder they can.

name: Laegrim Ranger
description: A ranger of the Laegrim, the Green-elves of the woods, warding the eaves of the forest realms. Silent woodsmen and deadly archers, they patrol the wild marches, shadow intruders and keep the old paths between the Elven lands.

name: Hunting Lizard
description: A swift, pack-hunting lizard of the warm lands, running down small game in coordinated rushes. Bold and persistent, a pack can pull down prey far larger than any single one of them.

name: Ice drake
description: Ice-drakes are Cold-drakes of the Earth element distinguished by an icy breath, dwelling in the frozen reaches of the far North.

name: Lynx
description: A medium-sized, tuft-eared wildcat of the northern forests, hunting hares, birds and small deer. Solitary and elusive, it keeps to dense cover and is more often heard than seen.

name: Water Mammals
description: The otters, minks, beavers and water-rats of river and lake-shore — sleek swimmers that fish, forage and burrow along the banks. Mostly shy of Men, their dams, slides and tracks mark every healthy waterway.

name: Lions
description: Great tawny cats of the warmer plains and scrublands of the South, hunting deer, wild cattle and the herds of Men. They hunt by ambush and stealth, and a hungry pride will not shrink from a lone traveller.

name: Cultirith Patrol
description: A patrol of soldiery based at the stronghold of Cultirith, warding its district against raiders and brigands, keeping the local roads and challenging strangers passing through their lord's lands.

name: Merchants & Traders
description: Traders plying their goods between towns and lands — cloth, metalwork, salt, wine and small luxuries, with a laden pony, a pack or a guarded wagon. Shrewd and talkative, they carry news as readily as wares and know the roads and prices of half a kingdom.

name: Minas Ithil Courier
description: A rider bearing messages to and from Minas Ithil — once a fair tower of Gondor, later the dread Minas Morgul. In the realm's good days an honest courier of the South Kingdom; in its evil days, a servant of the Nazgûl carrying word no honest man should read.

name: Young Trolls
description: Half-grown Trolls, not yet at full size or strength but already dangerous — clumsier and more reckless than their elders, and often driven from a lair to fend for themselves. Easier prey than a full-grown Troll, but a pack of them is no small peril.

description: A wild goat of the high crags, agile and sure-footed on cliffs that would daunt any predator. The rams bear heavy curved horns, and the herds keep to heights where few hunters can follow.

description: Rats of cellar, midden and ruin, thriving wherever folk leave food and filth. Quick-breeding and bold, they spoil stores, carry sickness, and in old tunnels and dungeons can swarm in alarming numbers.

name: Scout Elves
description: Elven scouts ranging the wild country about Rivendell and the Trollshaws, watching the fords and old roads of Rhudaur. They move unseen, mark the passage of Orcs and brigands, and carry word swiftly back to the Last Homely House.

name: Ooze
description: Treacherous soft ground — quicksand, quagmire, sucking mud or slime-filled pools — that grips and swallows whatever blunders in. The more a victim struggles, the deeper they sink.

name: Orc Elite Guards
description: Picked Orc warriors, bigger and better-armed than the common run, set to guard a captain, a fortress gate or some matter of the Enemy's; Sauron's own guards among them are grimly disciplined and far more dangerous than ordinary goblins.

name: Orc Females
description: Female Orcs, seldom seen abroad and kept mostly within the deep warrens where the breed is reared. Met in the tunnels of Moria and like strongholds, they are as vicious as the males in defense of their broods.

name: Orc Caravan
description: A train of Orcs hauling supplies, plunder or slaves across the Enemy's lands, plodding under whip and guard between Nurn's fields and the towers of Mordor. Slow and ill-tempered, it is heavily watched but vulnerable if caught in the open.

name: Orc Slaves & Guards
description: A column of wretched thralls — Men, lesser Orcs and other captives — driven to labor under the lash of Orc slave-drivers. The guards are brutal and watchful; the slaves, broken and desperate, may turn on their masters given the smallest chance.

name: Orc Warband
description: A war-band of Orcs on the march or raid — dozens of fighters with their captains, sometimes mounted or trailing a baggage-train. Bound for plunder, slaughter or muster, such a band is a serious threat to any small company that crosses its path.

name: Orc Warg-riders
description: Orcs mounted on Wargs — the great evil wolves of the North — fast, far-ranging and deadly. Used as raiders, outriders and shock troops, they strike at speed across open country and harry fugitives and caravans before melting back into the wild.

name: People Fur Trappers
description: Men who range the northern woods and wilds trapping beasts for their pelts — solitary, hardy and weather-worn, coming to town only to sell furs and lay in supplies before vanishing back into the trees.

name: People Hunters
description: Men who live by the chase, hunting game in forest, hill and fen for meat and hides. Skilled with bow and snare and at home in the wild, they range far from any settlement and read sign that townsfolk would never notice.

name: People Reed Peat Cutters
description: Marsh-folk who cut reed for thatch and dig peat for fuel from the bogs and fens, working the wetlands by flat-bottomed boat. Poor and little regarded, they know the safe paths through mires that swallow the unwary.

name: Pronghorns
description: Swift, antelope-like grazers of the open steppe and plain, among the fastest beasts of Middle-earth. They run in skittish herds and rely on speed and keen eyes to outpace any pursuer.

name: Elves
description: A chance meeting with Elves abroad in the wider lands — usually a small party on the road rather than the folk of a settled realm: travellers between the havens, a wandering company keeping to the dusk, or hunters far afield. Courteous but reserved with strangers, they seldom linger long.

name: Merchant Caravan
description: A train of merchants travelling together for safety — wagons, pack-beasts and hired guards strung along the trade-roads between the great markets. A rich and tempting prize for brigands, and so rarely met without an escort of armed men.

name: Pilgrim Wanderer
description: A lone traveller on a journey of faith or penance, bound for some shrine, holy place or vow far from home. Footsore and single-minded, pilgrims trust to the charity of those they meet and to the protection their purpose is thought to grant.

name: Pilgrim to Mithlond
description: An Elf making the last journey west, bound for the Grey Havens to take ship into the Undying Lands. Such pilgrims pass quietly through Lindon, alone or in small companies, their thoughts already half upon the sea and the long weariness of the Ages plain upon them.

name: Plague Refugee
description: Folk fleeing pestilence — driven from a stricken town or village, carrying what little they could and perhaps the sickness itself. Desperate and shunned, refugees beg shelter and food, and the wary turn them away for fear of what they bring.

name: Renegade Elf
description: An Elf who has forsaken his people — outlawed for some old deed, soured by grief, or gone solitary and strange in the wilder corners of Lindon. Wary and unpredictable, a renegade may aid a traveller as readily as rob one, and answers to no lord.

name: Poisonous Fumes
description: Foul or deadly vapours — marsh-gas, volcanic fumes or the airs of some tainted place — that sicken, stupefy or kill those who breathe them. Often unseen, they pool in hollows, caves and stagnant low ground.

name: Elven Noble
description: A high-born Elf of one of the great houses, travelling with attendants between the Elven realms on errands of state, kinship or lore. Met in Lindon, the Ered Luin and the eastern woods alike, such a lord or lady bears authority lightly but unmistakably.

name: Pike
description: The Pike is vicious, and is renowned as the shark of the freshwaters. It has a powerful jaw and wicked teeth, and once it has a hold it will not let go unless it is seriously wounded or it succeeds in biting off a healthy piece of its victim. Pike feed on crustaceans and shellfish when young, graduating to larger prey as they age. Adults eat fish and small mammals. Fortunately, Pike rarely attack a swimmer. This is particularly reassuring to the Rivermen of Calenardhon (Rohan), since Pike grow up to 7' in length in the Onodló (Entwash). Note: Once bitten, a victim will not be attacked again, but they must roll an additional attack to knock the fish loose, with no subtractions for DB, armor etc.

name: Pirates
description: Sea-raiders who prey on coastal shipping and settlements, putting in to plunder and slipping away with their spoil. Lawless and dangerous, they range the western and southern coasts, sometimes in league with the Corsairs of Umbar, sometimes for themselves alone.

name: Sea Captain Sailor
description: An Elven ship-master of Cirdan's folk, commanding one of the grey vessels that ply the Gulf of Lhun and the coasts of Harlindon. Long-sighted and sea-hardened, such captains carry messages, goods and passengers between the havens — and, at the last, those bound west.

name: Serganka
description: A rare bird of the eastern wilds, prized and little understood, met only in remote country. Wary and striking in plumage, it keeps far from the haunts of Men.

description: Men who live and work along the great rivers — boatmen, ferriers, fishers and traders who move goods and folk by water. They know every shoal, landing and backwater, and keep a shrewd independence from the lords of the dry land.

name: Sand glass storm
description: A blinding storm of wind-driven sand and grit that scours skin, chokes the lungs and erases the trail, common in the southern deserts. It can rob a caravan of its bearings and bury landmarks in hours.

name: Sailors
description: Seafaring and river-going men met in port or aboard ship — crewing trade-vessels, fishing boats and ferries. Hardy, superstitious and free with a tale, they know the coasts, currents and harbours of their waters.

name: Severe Weather
description: Killing weather — blizzards, ice-storms, hail, gales and freezing rain — that can fell the unsheltered traveller through cold and exhaustion alone. The high country and far North are deadliest when the sky turns.

name: Sindar Guardian
description: A Sindarin warden set to keep some threshold of the Elven lands — a ford, a forest-eaves, a hidden path. Silent and watchful, clad to melt into wood and shadow, the guardian challenges intruders and speeds friends quietly on their way.

name: Slaves and Guards
description: A coffle of slaves driven under armed guard — captives bound for the markets or the labor of some master, herded along the road by hard-eyed keepers. The guards are watchful and brutal; the slaves, broken but always alert for a chance to flee.

name: Southerners
description: Men of the southern lands — Haradrim and their neighbours, dark-haired and richly or strangely arrayed — met as traders, envoys, mercenaries or soldiers far north of their homes, and often eyed with mistrust in the lands of the West.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "Enedhwaith", "probability": "Extremely Hard"}, {"region": "Minhiriath", "probability": "Sheer Folly"}, {"region": "Mintyrnath", "probability": "Absurd"}, {"region": "Old Forest", "probability": "Very Hard"}, {"region": "Rast Vorn", "probability": "Absurd"}, {"region": "South Arthedain", "probability": "Absurd"}, {"region": "South Downs", "probability": "Absurd"}, {"region": "Tower Hills", "probability": "Sheer Folly"}]

name: Snow Leopard
description: A solitary, grey-dappled cat of the high cold peaks, superbly suited to crag and snow. A patient stalker of goats and sheep among the heights, shy of Men and rarely seen in the lowlands.

name: Trolls
description: Bred by Morgoth in mockery of Ents, Trolls are as tough and stupid as the stone from which they were made. Some feel they are related to Giants. In any case, Trolls hate all other creatures, a legacy of Morgoth's dark touch. Normal Trolls are divided into several types (based on their location): Hill, Cave, Snow, and Stone-trolls. The latter is the most common group. These types all revert to the stone of their substance when exposed to the light of day, for they were created in Darkness and the Sun can unmake them. Physical Character: They are huge and immensely strong, with thick bodies and limbs. Their tough hides have an inconsistent quality; many have overlapping scales and some have body hair. Various shades of brown, green, or grey hide, with black or brown eyes. They have black blood. Average endurance. 9 to 11 feet. Variable lifespan; hundreds of years. Trolls do not understand fear. Their vision is the same as that of Orcs. Sunlight. When exposed to the natural light of day they turn to stone. Trolls live in order to play and eat. To them play means killing and pillaging, and eating means fresh meat - raw or cooked - or jelly made from innards.

description: The small creatures of field, hedge and burrow — hares, foxes, squirrels, weasels, mice and the like. Seldom a threat, they are the everyday life of the land and the prey on which larger hunters depend.

name: Thieves & Smugglers
description: Footpads, cutpurses and smugglers who work the towns, fairs and trade-roads, moving stolen goods and contraband, slipping past tolls and gates, and lightening the purses of the unwary. Quick-fingered and quicker to vanish, they answer to whoever pays.

description: Pale, cold-hardened Trolls of the northern wastes and high snows, ranging Forochel and the frozen reaches. White as the drifts they hunt in and thick-hided against the cold, they are among the few Trolls that brave the daylight of the polar lands.

name: Snow Lions
description: Pale, thick-furred great cats of the northern mountains and snow-fields, well camouflaged against ice and stone. Powerful ambush hunters of goats, deer and elk, they are seldom seen until they strike.

name: Tereg Trolls of Dol Guldur
description: Trolls kept and bred at Dol Guldur in southern Mirkwood, hardened to the Necromancer's service. Set to guard the hill of sorcery and its approaches, they are crueller and better-handled than wild Trolls, and are loosed on intruders and on the Wood-elves and Woodmen of the southern forest.

name: Spiders
description: The many spiders of wood, cave and ruin, most small and harmless but some grown large and venomous in dark, tainted places. Their webs choke old passages and forest hollows, and the bigger kinds take birds and small beasts.

name: Spies
description: Agents abroad on someone's secret business — watching roads and garrisons, carrying word, or worming into the confidence of the powerful. They pass as travellers, merchants or servants, and a careless one is as dangerous to its master as to its mark.

name: Tribesmen
description: Folk of a clan or tribe living beyond the settled realms — hillmen, marsh-dwellers, herders or hunters bound by kin and custom rather than king. Proud and wary of outsiders, and fierce in defense of their own ground.

name: The Witch king
description: The Lord of the Nazgul, the Witch-king who ruled the realm of Angmar against the Dunedain of the North and later led Sauron's forces from Minas Morgul; mightiest of the nine Ringwraiths.

name: Toads
description: Frogs, toads and other amphibians of pond, marsh and damp wood, loud in the breeding season and harmless to all but insects. Some marsh-toads carry an acrid skin-poison that sickens any beast foolish enough to mouth them.

name: Wandering Company
description: A band of folk travelling the roads together — players, pilgrims, displaced families or companions on a long road, sharing fire and protection in numbers. Their fortunes and friendliness vary, but a company on the move always carries news from afar.

name: War Wolves
description: Huge dogs (S. Ormaraka, aka Yelmaraka), often mistaken for Wargs, bred and trained for combat and frequently fielded in numbers as trackers, scouts or to harass enemy armies. The War-wolves of Waw serve as combat units, while those of Angmar are used as mounts by Orcish raiders and Orc-lords. They look like merely overlarge Wolves, which is true, but they are bolder, far more violent and much more cunning.

name: Wild Hobbits
description: Hobbits living rough and un-Shirelike at the edges of settled country — in the eaves of the Old Forest, the Bree-land hedges and the lonely downs. Warier and more weather-hardened than their kin, they trust no big folk and vanish into bracken and burrow at the first sign of trouble.

name: Woodland birds of Rhosgobel
description: The many woodland birds that gather about Rhosgobel, home of Radagast the Brown, who befriends and speaks with them. Quick to carry word through the forest, they serve as eyes and messengers for the bird-tamer of the Wood.

name: Leeches
description: Blood-sucking worms of still and sluggish water, fastening unseen to any warm-blooded thing that wades the shallows. A nuisance of fen and marsh, and in foul waters they gather thick enough to weaken a wading beast.

name: Solitary Dwarf
description: A single Dwarf met far from any hold — a wanderer, exile, craftsman seeking work, or one bent on a private errand or grudge. Gruff with strangers but steadfast to those who earn it, such travellers turn up almost anywhere in the wide lands.

name: Angusaiweli
description: Found virtually anywhere in Middle-earth, save in the coldest climes, the Angusaiwë (Q. "Iron Unbirds"; pl. "Angusaiwéli") is the most resilient, advanced, and common of the larger Rávatsar. This is due to the fact that they are, unlike any other reptile or amphibian, warm-blooded. The Angusaiwéli constitute a unique branch of the Rávatsar and are closely related to flightless birds; thus their name. There are nine species: those that run on four legs (three species), those that run on two legs (five species), and those that hop (one species). All are deep grey or black with bright red eyes. The two-legged species walk upright using their tails for balance and can travel over rugged terrain at speeds exceeding 35 miles an hour. Poisonous carnivores, Unbirds hunt lizards, frogs, birds, large insects, and small mammals, swallowing smaller prey whole. Against larger foes they bare hollow, needle-like fangs and inject a potent (3rd lvl) venom that immobilizes (1-100 rds with RR failure 01-65) or kills (1-10 rds with RR failure 66+). Aside from their speed, they rely on cunning and horny body armor, with a ridge of pointed scales and scales as tough as iron.

description: One of the roving companies of Elves that cross the lands at dusk, singing as they go, much as Gildor's folk once passed through the Shire. They keep to the old paths between the havens and the eastern realms, share fire and counsel with worthy travellers, and are gone by morning.

name: Travellers
description: Folk upon the road for reasons of their own — bound for market or kin, fleeing hard times, or simply going from one town to the next. Usually harmless and glad of company on a lonely stretch, though the road draws all sorts and not every traveller is what they seem.

description: A mine or quarry cut into hill and rock — galleries, shafts, spoil-heaps and stone-faces, worked or long abandoned. A source of metal and stone, and of cave-ins, foul air and whatever has crept into the empty workings.

name: Kirinkir
description: The Kirinki is a tiny scarlet bird with an extremely high chirp that was one of the most beloved songbirds of Numenor. After the Downfall in S.A. 3319, knowledge of these wondrous singers became confined to verse, illustrations in books, and works of art depicting the little creatures. Some of the Faithful who escaped from Numenor, however, brought their pet Kirinkir with them. Whether any survived or thrived is another matter, although there have been rumors that Radagast the Brown has a pair at his home at Rhosgobel (near the southwestern eaves of Mirkwood).

name: Sarnúmeni
description: Distantly related to the Carnantor, the Sarnúmeni (Q. "West-stones"; sing. "Sarnúmen") are large, hard-skinned, freshwater Eels. Their name is derived from their grey, horny scales, which lend a Sarnúmen tremendous protection and enable them to burrow in riverbeds, camouflaging themselves as rocks. Since the mid-Second Age, when they came from their original home in Númenor and were carelessly introduced into the rivers and lakes of southern Endor, they have become the dominant hunter in the watershed of the Yellow Mountains (S. "Ered Laranor"; Q. "Orolanari"). Aside from great strength and huge teeth, Sarnúmeni are noted for having deadly barbs along their spine. These barbs are connected with poison glands, and anyone stepping on or handling one of these formidable Eels is in danger of being injected with a bluish nerve poison named for the Sarnúmen. Herbalists have been known to refine this liquid, creating a lethal (30th) poison that is feared all over southern Middle-earth. Fortunately, the liquid has an exceedingly limited life of its own and is extremely hard (-30) to handle without exposing oneself to its effects.

name: Fintónarki
description: The Fintónarki, or Wild Goats, are odd beasts found on the plains south of Mirkwood. They have several characteristics that set them apart from the other Goats of Middle-earth. For one thing, they are sometimes found in mixed herds with the Wild Horses of the area, an arrangement found nowhere else. Second, they are almost untamable, although it is reported that Elves may have some success at this task. Third, they are immune to all naturally occuring ingestible poisons and hence can live on almost anything that contains nourishment. Finally, these strange creatures have an unquenchable desire to be scratched on the head, between their long, dangerous horns. It is rumored among various denizens of the Talath Harroch region (particularly those with small, naïve children) that if one should do one of these hoofed hedonists the favor of scratching it in the proper spot, it will become ever loyal to the person who scratches it. This tale has caused numerous injuries to youngsters seeking an unusual pet.

name: Rárorassi
description: The largest and most powerful of Endor's Sheep, the Rútorasse (Q. "Eastern Woolhorns"; pl. "Rútorassi") is a stout, black and silver beast that stands about 4-5 feet at the shoulder. It is aptly named, for its silvery head fur grows up the sides of its curved horns. These two forward-facing weapons can be lethal, and the Woolhorn is quick to employ them for defense; thus, this beast is difficult to domesticate. Native to the lands of Dalpygis, Chey, and the Kargagis Ahar in central Middle-earth, Woolhorns roam at night in small, segregated herds. Males run apart from the females except during the mating season, which lasts from late summer until early fall. When united, the mixed herd is relatively stationary for about a month, the first half of which is devoted to courting rituals, including brutal duels between males seeking dominance (and their choice of mates).

name: Snowhare
description: The snow hare is one of the largest members of the rabbit family. It inhabits the upper elevations of the Misty Mountains where there is snow cover year round. The animal's silvery white pelt, prized for its warmth, provides excellent camouflage for the furry mountain denizen. A larger, more fearsome variety of the snow hare has been rumored to exist lurking close to the opening of high mountain caves. These creatures seem to exhibit a cruel intelligence and are rumored to frenzy upon provocation. This description was given by a traveller after returning half-crazed from the mountains: "It had huge ... well I mean gigantic ... just big teeth."

name: Lisicavi
description: Small, large-eared desert fox (Ap. Nightears, pl. Lisicavi) found in Harad, thriving in the cool of the desert night. It hunts in pairs or small family groups for rodents and large insects and digs out sleeping reptiles. It is hunted for its brilliant auburn coat.

name: Foxes
description: Wild foxes of wood, hill and field — sharp-witted solitary hunters of rabbits, fowl, mice and carrion. Bold around farms and middens, they slip away at the first sign of danger and are more often known by their tracks and raided hen-roosts than by sight.

name: Cattle Herds
description: Herds of cattle pastured on meadow and river-plain, raised for beef, hides, milk and draught. Driven to market along the droving roads, they are a mainstay of local wealth and an easy prize for rustlers.

name: Horse Herds
description: Herds of horses bred and grazed on the open grasslands, prized for riding, war and trade. Watched by mounted herders, the finest stock is jealously guarded and ranks among the region's most valued exports.

name: Sheep Pastures
description: Flocks of sheep grazing the hills, downs and rough pasture for wool, milk and meat. Shepherds and their dogs work the high ground through the seasons, and the wool-clip is a cornerstone of local trade.

name: Apiaries
description: Kept hives and wild bee-trees worked for honey and wax — a sweet and valuable harvest where sugar is unknown. Bee-keepers tend their skeps in meadow and orchard, and a good honey-year is cause for celebration.

name: Fisheries
description: Waters worked for their fish — rivers, lakes and coasts where folk net, trap and line their catch. Drying-racks, weirs and fishing-boats mark the settlements, and salt or smoked fish keeps the region fed through winter.

name: Timberlands
description: Worked woodlands felled for timber and firewood, the logs hauled and floated downriver to mill and market. Woodcutters' camps and sawpits dot the forest edge, and good timber is a steady source of wealth.

name: Nut Groves
description: Groves and wild stands harvested for nuts — beech-mast, hazel and walnut — gathered in autumn for food, oil and trade. The mast also fattens the pigs turned loose to forage in the woods.

name: Orchards & Wild Fruit
description: Orchards and wild fruit-lands yielding apples, plums, berries or olives in season — gathered fresh, dried, pressed or brewed. A welcome bounty that draws foragers, birds and the occasional bear.

name: Herb-lands
description: Meadows, woods and tended gardens worked for herbs — for medicine, cooking, dye and craft. Herb-wives know the right seasons and places, and healing simples from such lands are valued far beyond the region.

name: Mines & Diggings
description: Workings cut into hill and mountain for metal and stone — iron, tin, copper, lead, silver, gems or mithril, hewn in shaft and gallery. Spoil-heaps, smelters and guarded ore-roads mark the diggings, and the deeper delvings can wake older perils.
